View problem statement
Problem statement
Design the core of a text editor that supports inserting, deleting, and replacing text, with full undo and redo. The emphasis is on clean object-oriented modeling of reversible operations.

Core Requirements:

Edit: insert text at a position, delete a range, replace a range.
Undo: revert the last edit, restoring the exact previous state.
Redo: reapply the last undone edit; a fresh edit after an undo clears the redo history.
Validation: reject out-of-range positions; report nothing-to-undo / nothing-to-redo clearly.

Additional Considerations:

Undo/redo should be O(1) per step with unbounded history (carry the inverse, not a full snapshot per keystroke).
Single-user editor: concurrency is not the focus, but operations must stay consistent if accessed from multiple threads.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. TextEditor, Document, EditCommand, Memento).
Model each edit as a Command that knows how to execute and undo itself; use a Memento for snapshot fallback.
Discuss design patterns (Command, Memento).
Treat collaborative editing (OT / CRDT) as an extensibility direction, not the core.

# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Text Editor with Undo / Redo.

The "nouns" of the system live here:

* `Document`  вАФ the one mutable thing: a text buffer plus a caret (cursor).
                It exposes two low-level primitives, `_apply_insert` and
                `_apply_delete`, that the Command objects call. Nothing else is
                allowed to touch the raw buffer, so every mutation funnels
                through one validated door.
* `Memento`   вАФ a frozen snapshot of the buffer + cursor. It is the *fallback*
                undo mechanism: for an edit whose inverse is not cheap to carry
                (an opaque whole-buffer transform), we snapshot first and
                restore on undo. The three built-in edits don't need it вАФ they
                carry a cheap inverse вАФ but the snapshot path is real and tested.
* Exceptions  вАФ one base, `EditorError`, with a specific subclass per failure
                mode so callers catch exactly the thing they expect.

рЯОЩпЄП "There's really only one piece of mutable state вАФ the Document. Everything
else (the commands, the editor's two stacks) is bookkeeping around it. So I make
the Document guard its own buffer and expose just two primitives the commands
drive."
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass


# вФАвФА EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
class EditorError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises вАФ callers catch one type."""


class InvalidPositionError(EditorError):
    """A position or length falls outside the buffer (out-of-range edit)."""


class NothingToUndoError(EditorError):
    """undo() was called but the undo stack is empty."""


class NothingToRedoError(EditorError):
    """redo() was called but the redo stack is empty (or a fresh edit cleared it)."""


# вФАвФА MEMENTO: the snapshot value object (Memento pattern) вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Memento:
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # A read-only photograph of the whole document at one instant: the text and
    # where the caret was. Frozen on purpose вАФ once taken, a snapshot can never
    # drift, so restoring it is guaranteed to reproduce the exact past state.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "The Memento is just a frozen pair: the text and the cursor. It's the
    #     'save game' I can always reload to get back an exact previous state."
    text: str
    cursor: int


# вФАвФА DOCUMENT: the single mutable buffer + caret вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
class Document:
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The only object that owns the raw text. It enforces ONE rule: every
    # position touched must land inside the buffer. Because the two primitives
    # `_apply_insert` / `_apply_delete` are the *only* way to change the text,
    # there is exactly one place to get range-checking right.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "The Document is the source of truth. I deliberately gave it tiny
    #     primitives вАФ insert-a-string-here, delete-a-span-here вАФ and let the
    #     Command objects compose them. The editor never pokes the buffer directly."
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        self._text = text
        # The caret sits between characters; valid range is 0..len(text).
        self._cursor = len(text)

    # вФАвФА queries вФАвФА
    @property
    def text(self) -> str:
        return self._text

    @property
    def cursor(self) -> int:
        return self._cursor

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self._text)

    # вФАвФА validation (the one rule this class guards) вФАвФА
    def _validate_position(self, pos: int, length: int = 0) -> None:
        # WHY: a caret position is valid anywhere from 0 (before the first char)
        #      to len (after the last char). A span additionally must not run
        #      off the end. One helper, every primitive reuses it.
        if length < 0:
            raise InvalidPositionError(f"length must be >= 0, got {length}")

        if pos < 0 or pos > len(self._text):
            raise InvalidPositionError(
                f"position {pos} out of range [0, {len(self._text)}]"
            )

        if pos + length > len(self._text):
            raise InvalidPositionError(
                f"span [{pos}, {pos + length}) runs past end {len(self._text)}"
            )

    # вФАвФА low-level primitives (the ONLY things Command objects call) вФАвФА
    def _apply_insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        # WHY: splice the new text in at `pos`. Caret lands just after what we
        #      typed вАФ exactly where a human's cursor sits after typing.
        self._validate_position(pos)
        self._text = self._text[:pos] + text + self._text[pos:]
        self._cursor = pos + len(text)

    def _apply_delete(self, pos: int, length: int) -> str:
        # WHY: cut the span out AND return it. Returning the removed substring is
        #      what lets DeleteCommand carry its own cheap inverse вАФ undo just
        #      re-inserts this exact text.
        self._validate_position(pos, length)
        removed = self._text[pos : pos + length]
        self._text = self._text[:pos] + self._text[pos + length :]
        self._cursor = pos
        return removed

    def _move_cursor(self, pos: int) -> None:
        # WHY: undo restores not just the text but the EXACT caret it had before
        #      the edit, so "exact previous state" is literally true. Validated
        #      like any other position.
        self._validate_position(pos)
        self._cursor = pos

    # вФАвФА Memento hooks (snapshot-based undo fallback) вФАвФА
    def snapshot(self) -> Memento:
        # WHY: capture text + caret as a frozen Memento before an opaque edit.
        return Memento(text=self._text, cursor=self._cursor)

    def restore(self, memento: Memento) -> None:
        # WHY: reload a snapshot wholesale. This is the fallback undo path used
        #      by SnapshotCommand for transforms with no cheap inverse.
        self._text = memento.text
        self._cursor = memento.cursor

# Python 3.11+
"""The Command hierarchy вАФ the pluggable, reversible-operation seam.

Every edit the user can make is modelled as an `EditCommand`: an object that
knows how to `execute` itself on a Document AND how to `undo` itself exactly.
That is the whole trick behind cheap undo/redo вАФ the editor keeps two stacks of
these objects and never reasons about *how* to reverse an edit; each command
already knows.

Two flavours of command live here:

* `InsertCommand` / `DeleteCommand` / `ReplaceCommand` вАФ carry a *cheap inverse*.
  On execute they capture just enough information (the removed substring, the
  inserted length) to reverse themselves in O(edit-size) memory. No
  whole-document snapshot per keystroke.
* `SnapshotCommand` вАФ the Memento-based *fallback* for an edit whose inverse is
  not cheap to carry (an opaque whole-buffer transform). It snapshots the
  document before and after, and undo/redo just restore those snapshots.

рЯОЩпЄП "I make every edit a little object that can run itself forward and backward.
The editor just pushes and pops these вАФ it never writes a single 'if it was an
insert, delete it' branch. Adding a new kind of edit is a new class, nothing in
the editor changes."
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from collections.abc import Callable

from models import Document, Memento


class EditCommand(ABC):
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The abstraction the TextEditor depends on (Dependency Inversion). The
    # editor's undo/redo stacks hold `EditCommand`s вАФ they never know which
    # concrete edit they're reversing. That's the seam: new edit types plug in
    # with zero editor changes.
    @abstractmethod
    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        """Apply this edit to the document (also used to *redo* it)."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    @abstractmethod
    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        """Reverse this edit, restoring the document to its pre-execute state."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class InsertCommand(EditCommand):
    """Insert `text` at `pos`. Inverse: delete the same span back out."""

    def __init__(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._text = text
        self._cursor_before = 0  # captured on execute so undo restores the exact caret

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: remember where the caret was, THEN insert. If the position is bad,
        #      `_apply_insert` raises before we've recorded anything.
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._text)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: the inverse of "insert N chars at pos" is "delete N chars at pos".
        #      We know N = len(text) without storing a snapshot.
        doc._apply_delete(self._pos, len(self._text))
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)


class DeleteCommand(EditCommand):
    """Delete `length` chars at `pos`. Inverse: re-insert the removed substring."""

    def __init__(self, pos: int, length: int) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._length = length
        self._removed = ""  # the inverse info, captured on execute
        self._cursor_before = 0

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: `_apply_delete` returns the exact substring it cut. We keep it вАФ
        #      that string IS our cheap inverse, no document snapshot needed.
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        self._removed = doc._apply_delete(self._pos, self._length)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed)
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)


class ReplaceCommand(EditCommand):
    """Replace `length` chars at `pos` with `text` (a delete + an insert).

    Inverse info is the removed substring; undo reverses the two steps in
    opposite order: pull the inserted text back out, then restore the old text.
    """

    def __init__(self, pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._length = length
        self._text = text
        self._removed = ""
        self._cursor_before = 0

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: replace is just two primitives in a row. Doing the delete first
        #      means the insert position is always valid (the buffer only shrank).
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        self._removed = doc._apply_delete(self._pos, self._length)
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._text)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: undo runs the two steps in REVERSE order вАФ first remove the text we
        #      inserted, then put back the text we deleted. Order matters: do it
        #      the other way and the positions no longer line up.
        doc._apply_delete(self._pos, len(self._text))
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed)
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)


class SnapshotCommand(EditCommand):
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The Memento-based fallback. Some edits have no cheap inverse вАФ e.g. "run
    # this opaque transform over the whole buffer". For those we can't carry a
    # small inverse, so we snapshot the document before and after and let undo /
    # redo just restore the right frozen photo. Same EditCommand contract, so it
    # rides the SAME undo/redo stacks as the cheap commands.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "When an edit isn't cheaply reversible, I fall back to the classic
    #     Memento: snapshot before, snapshot after, and undo/redo flip between
    #     the two saved states. It costs whole-document memory, so I only use it
    #     where carrying an inverse isn't possible."
    def __init__(self, transform: Callable[[str], str]) -> None:
        self._transform = transform
        self._before: Memento | None = None
        self._after: Memento | None = None

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: first run computes the transform and remembers both snapshots;
        #      a later redo just restores the cached 'after' вАФ no re-running the
        #      transform, so it works even if the transform isn't repeatable.
        if self._after is None:
            self._before = doc.snapshot()
            new_text = self._transform(doc.text)
            self._after = Memento(text=new_text, cursor=len(new_text))

        doc.restore(self._after)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # The editor only ever undoes a command it already executed and pushed,
        # so `_before` is always set here; the assert documents that contract.
        assert self._before is not None
        doc.restore(self._before)

# Python 3.11+
"""TextEditor вАФ the orchestrator вАФ and its thread-safe subclass.

The editor owns three things: the one `Document`, an **undo stack**, and a
**redo stack**. Every mutating call follows the same tiny recipe:

    build a Command  ->  execute it on the document  ->  push it onto undo,
    and clear redo (a fresh edit kills the redo future)

`undo` pops the undo stack, runs the command's `undo`, and pushes it to redo;
`redo` is the mirror image. Because the commands carry their own inverses, the
editor contains ZERO "how do I reverse this?" logic вАФ it just moves objects
between two stacks.

Concurrency note (honest): a text editor is a single-user, essentially
single-threaded domain. There's no real contention to design around. But if the
same editor is *touched* from more than one thread, the document and the two
stacks must move together or they'll desynchronise. So the thread-safe version
guards every operation with ONE editor lock via the no-op `_lock` hook. That's
sufficient and correct here; see ¬І9 for why a single lock is the right call and
why real collaborative editing (OT / CRDT) is a different, larger problem.
"""

import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    Document,
    EditorError,
    NothingToRedoError,
    NothingToUndoError,
)
from strategies import (
    DeleteCommand,
    EditCommand,
    InsertCommand,
    ReplaceCommand,
)


class TextEditor:
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The public API. It coordinates the Document and the two command stacks,
    # and it is the only place that decides WHEN redo gets cleared. It never
    # mutates the buffer directly and never branches on edit type вАФ it builds a
    # Command and lets the Command do the work.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "The editor is a thin coordinator. Each edit becomes a Command that
    #     runs and records itself; undo and redo are just popping one stack and
    #     pushing the other. The cleverness is in the Command, not here."
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        self._document = Document(text)
        self._undo_stack: list[EditCommand] = []
        self._redo_stack: list[EditCommand] = []

    # вФАвФА the concurrency hook (Template Method) вФАвФА
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does NOTHING in the clean core вАФ there are zero lock imports in
        #      the logic below. The thread-safe subclass overrides just this one
        #      method with a real lock, so the diff to make the editor safe is a
        #      handful of lines and the core stays readable.
        yield

    # вФАвФА mutating operations: build a command, run it, record it вФАвФА
    def insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        # WHY: execute BEFORE recording. If the position is invalid, execute
        #      raises InvalidPositionError and we never touch the stacks вАФ a
        #      rejected edit leaves history exactly as it was.
        with self._lock():
            command = InsertCommand(pos, text)
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._record(command)

    def delete(self, pos: int, length: int) -> None:
        with self._lock():
            command = DeleteCommand(pos, length)
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._record(command)

    def replace(self, pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None:
        with self._lock():
            command = ReplaceCommand(pos, length, text)
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._record(command)

    # вФАвФА undo / redo: move one command between the two stacks вФАвФА
    def undo(self) -> None:
        # WHY: pop the last edit, reverse it, and park it on the redo stack so a
        #      later redo can replay it. Empty stack is a clear, typed error вАФ
        #      not a silent no-op вАФ so the caller knows there was nothing to undo.
        with self._lock():
            if not self._undo_stack:
                raise NothingToUndoError("there is no edit to undo")

            command = self._undo_stack.pop()
            command.undo(self._document)
            self._redo_stack.append(command)

    def redo(self) -> None:
        # WHY: the exact mirror of undo. Re-executing the command reproduces the
        #      original edit, then it goes back onto the undo stack.
        with self._lock():
            if not self._redo_stack:
                raise NothingToRedoError("there is no edit to redo")

            command = self._redo_stack.pop()
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._undo_stack.append(command)

    # вФАвФА query вФАвФА
    def text(self) -> str:
        with self._lock():
            return self._document.text

    # вФАвФА private helper вФАвФА
    def _record(self, command: EditCommand) -> None:
        # WHY: this is the rule that makes redo behave like every real editor:
        #      a brand-new edit invalidates the "redo future", so we clear it.
        #      Type a character after undoing and you can no longer redo вАФ the
        #      timeline forked.
        self._undo_stack.append(command)
        self._redo_stack.clear()


class ThreadSafeTextEditor(TextEditor):
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The only change needed to make the editor safe across threads: override
    # the no-op `_lock` with one real lock that wraps each whole operation.
    # The document and the two stacks then always move together вАФ no thread can
    # ever observe a half-applied edit (a torn buffer or a stack that doesn't
    # match the text).
    # рЯОЩпЄП "One lock around each operation. An edit either fully happens or another
    #     thread waits вАФ there's no in-between state to corrupt. I use an RLock
    #     defensively so a future refactor that nests two ops won't self-deadlock,
    #     though with today's code a plain Lock would do."
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        super().__init__(text)
        self._mutex = threading.RLock()

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._mutex:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Text Editor with Undo / Redo.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK".
"""

import threading

from editor_service import TextEditor, ThreadSafeTextEditor
from models import (
    Document,
    InvalidPositionError,
    NothingToRedoError,
    NothingToUndoError,
)
from strategies import SnapshotCommand


def main() -> None:
    # вФАвФА Case 1: the three edits build up a buffer вФАвФА
    ed = TextEditor()
    ed.insert(0, "Hello")
    ed.insert(5, " World")          # append at the end
    ed.delete(0, 6)                 # drop "Hello "
    ed.replace(0, 5, "Earth")       # "World" -> "Earth"
    print(f"Case 1: after insert/insert/delete/replace -> {ed.text()!r}")
    assert ed.text() == "Earth"
    print("Case 1: insert, delete, and replace each edit the buffer -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 2: undo walks history backward, exactly вФАвФА
    ed.undo()                       # undo replace -> "World"
    assert ed.text() == "World", ed.text()
    ed.undo()                       # undo delete  -> "Hello World"
    assert ed.text() == "Hello World", ed.text()
    print(f"Case 2: two undos restore -> {ed.text()!r}")
    print("Case 2: undo reverses edits and restores the exact previous text -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 3: redo replays what undo reversed вФАвФА
    ed.redo()                       # redo delete  -> "World"
    ed.redo()                       # redo replace -> "Earth"
    assert ed.text() == "Earth", ed.text()
    print(f"Case 3: two redos replay -> {ed.text()!r}")
    print("Case 3: redo reapplies undone edits in order -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 4: a fresh edit after undo clears the redo future вФАвФА
    ed2 = TextEditor("abc")
    ed2.insert(3, "d")              # "abcd"
    ed2.undo()                      # back to "abc"
    ed2.insert(3, "X")              # fresh edit -> "abcX", redo timeline forks
    assert ed2.text() == "abcX"
    try:
        ed2.redo()
        assert False, "redo should have been cleared by the fresh edit"
    except NothingToRedoError as e:
        print(f"Case 4: redo after a fresh edit -> {type(e).__name__}")
    print("Case 4: a new edit after undo throws away the redo stack -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 5: out-of-range edits are rejected, history untouched вФАвФА
    ed3 = TextEditor("abc")
    try:
        ed3.insert(99, "z")
        assert False, "should reject out-of-range insert"
    except InvalidPositionError as e:
        print(f"Case 5: insert at 99 into a 3-char doc -> {type(e).__name__}")
    try:
        ed3.undo()                  # nothing was recorded, so nothing to undo
        assert False, "should have nothing to undo"
    except NothingToUndoError as e:
        print(f"Case 5: undo on empty history -> {type(e).__name__}")
    assert ed3.text() == "abc"
    print("Case 5: bad positions raise a typed error and leave history clean -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 6: the Memento fallback вАФ an opaque transform undone by snapshot вФАвФА
    doc = Document("hello")
    upper = SnapshotCommand(transform=str.upper)
    upper.execute(doc)              # snapshot 'hello', jump to 'HELLO'
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"
    upper.undo(doc)                 # restore the frozen 'hello' snapshot
    assert doc.text == "hello"
    upper.execute(doc)              # redo restores the cached 'HELLO' snapshot
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"
    print(f"Case 6: snapshot-based transform undo/redo -> {doc.text!r}")
    print("Case 6: the Memento fallback reverses an edit with no cheap inverse -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 7: many threads insert under one lock вАФ no torn buffer, no lost edit вФАвФА
    n = 50
    safe = ThreadSafeTextEditor()
    start = threading.Barrier(n)

    def edit() -> None:
        start.wait()                # all n hit insert at the same instant
        safe.insert(0, "x")         # insert at 0 is always valid

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=edit) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    print(f"Case 7: {n} concurrent inserts -> len={len(safe.text())}, undo_depth={len(safe._undo_stack)}")
    assert len(safe.text()) == n            # every insert landed, none lost
    assert len(safe._undo_stack) == n       # one command recorded per edit
    print("Case 7: the editor lock serializes edits вАФ buffer and history stay consistent -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Text Editor with Undo / Redo.

Covers: each public edit (insert / delete / replace), every typed exception path,
undo-then-redo exactness, undo past the start, the redo-cleared-by-fresh-edit
rule, replace = delete + insert correctness, the Memento snapshot fallback, and a
modest concurrency proof (N threads insert under one lock вАФ no lost edits, no torn
buffer) using a threading.Barrier so all threads race at one instant.
"""

import threading

import pytest

from editor_service import TextEditor, ThreadSafeTextEditor
from models import (
    Document,
    InvalidPositionError,
    Memento,
    NothingToRedoError,
    NothingToUndoError,
)
from strategies import (
    DeleteCommand,
    InsertCommand,
    ReplaceCommand,
    SnapshotCommand,
)


# вФАвФА happy path: one test per public edit вФАвФА
def test_insert_at_position():
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")
    ed.insert(5, ",")
    assert ed.text() == "Hello, World"


def test_delete_range():
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")
    ed.delete(5, 6)                       # remove " World"
    assert ed.text() == "Hello"


def test_replace_range():
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")
    ed.replace(6, 5, "Earth")             # "World" -> "Earth"
    assert ed.text() == "Hello Earth"


def test_text_query_reflects_initial_content():
    assert TextEditor("seed").text() == "seed"


# вФАвФА replace == delete + insert (correctness of the compound command) вФАвФА
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "start,pos,length,text,expected",
    [
        ("Hello World", 6, 5, "Earth", "Hello Earth"),  # same-length swap
        ("abcdef", 1, 3, "X", "aXef"),                   # shrink
        ("abc", 1, 1, "YYYY", "aYYYYc"),                 # grow
        ("abc", 1, 0, "Z", "aZbc"),                      # zero-length == pure insert
    ],
)
def test_replace_equals_delete_then_insert(start, pos, length, text, expected):
    via_replace = TextEditor(start)
    via_replace.replace(pos, length, text)

    via_steps = TextEditor(start)
    via_steps.delete(pos, length)
    via_steps.insert(pos, text)

    assert via_replace.text() == expected
    assert via_steps.text() == expected


# вФАвФА typed exception paths вФАвФА
@pytest.mark.parametrize("pos", [-1, 12])
def test_insert_out_of_range_raises(pos):
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")        # length 11, valid caret 0..11
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.insert(pos, "x")


@pytest.mark.parametrize("pos,length", [(10, 5), (-1, 2), (0, 99)])
def test_delete_out_of_range_raises(pos, length):
    ed = TextEditor("Hello")
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.delete(pos, length)


def test_replace_out_of_range_raises():
    ed = TextEditor("abc")
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.replace(2, 5, "z")             # span runs past the end


def test_undo_on_empty_history_raises():
    with pytest.raises(NothingToUndoError):
        TextEditor("abc").undo()


def test_redo_on_empty_history_raises():
    with pytest.raises(NothingToRedoError):
        TextEditor("abc").redo()


def test_rejected_edit_leaves_history_untouched():
    ed = TextEditor("abc")
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.insert(99, "z")
    assert ed.text() == "abc"
    with pytest.raises(NothingToUndoError):   # nothing was recorded
        ed.undo()


# вФАвФА undo / redo exactness вФАвФА
def test_undo_then_redo_restores_exactly():
    ed = TextEditor("")
    ed.insert(0, "Hello")
    ed.insert(5, " World")
    ed.delete(0, 6)
    ed.replace(0, 5, "Earth")
    assert ed.text() == "Earth"

    history = ["World", "Hello World", "Hello", ""]
    for expected in history:              # undo all the way back to empty
        ed.undo()
        assert ed.text() == expected

    forward = ["Hello", "Hello World", "World", "Earth"]
    for expected in forward:              # redo all the way forward again
        ed.redo()
        assert ed.text() == expected


def test_undo_past_start_raises_after_draining_history():
    ed = TextEditor("")
    ed.insert(0, "a")
    ed.undo()
    assert ed.text() == ""
    with pytest.raises(NothingToUndoError):
        ed.undo()                          # one step past the start


def test_fresh_edit_after_undo_clears_redo():
    ed = TextEditor("abc")
    ed.insert(3, "d")                      # "abcd"
    ed.undo()                              # back to "abc"
    ed.insert(3, "X")                      # fresh edit forks the timeline
    assert ed.text() == "abcX"
    with pytest.raises(NothingToRedoError):
        ed.redo()                          # the "abcd" redo is gone


def test_undo_restores_exact_cursor():
    ed = TextEditor("abcdef")
    ed.insert(3, "ZZZ")                    # caret now after the insert
    ed.undo()
    # "exact previous state" includes the caret: it was at the end (6) before.
    assert ed.text() == "abcdef"
    assert ed._document.cursor == 6


# вФАвФА Memento snapshot fallback (Document + SnapshotCommand) вФАвФА
def test_document_snapshot_and_restore():
    doc = Document("hello")
    snap = doc.snapshot()
    assert isinstance(snap, Memento)
    doc._apply_insert(5, "!!!")
    assert doc.text == "hello!!!"
    doc.restore(snap)
    assert doc.text == "hello" and doc.cursor == snap.cursor


def test_snapshot_command_undo_redo():
    doc = Document("hello")
    cmd = SnapshotCommand(transform=str.upper)
    cmd.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"
    cmd.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "hello"
    cmd.execute(doc)                       # redo uses the cached 'after' snapshot
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"


# вФАвФА command-level inverse correctness вФАвФА
def test_delete_command_carries_its_inverse():
    doc = Document("abcdef")
    cmd = DeleteCommand(2, 3)              # remove "cde"
    cmd.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abf"
    cmd.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abcdef"


def test_insert_and_replace_commands_round_trip():
    doc = Document("abc")
    ins = InsertCommand(3, "XYZ")
    ins.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abcXYZ"
    rep = ReplaceCommand(0, 3, "Q")
    rep.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "QXYZ"
    rep.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abcXYZ"
    ins.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abc"


# вФАвФА modest concurrency proof (Barrier => all threads insert at one instant) вФАвФА
def test_concurrent_inserts_under_lock_lose_nothing():
    """N threads each insert one char under the editor lock.

    The buffer length must equal the number of inserts (no lost update / torn
    buffer) and the undo stack must hold exactly one command per edit.
    """
    n = 50
    ed = ThreadSafeTextEditor()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def attempt() -> None:
        barrier.wait()                     # all n race into insert simultaneously
        ed.insert(0, "x")                  # position 0 is always valid

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert len(ed.text()) == n             # every insert landed
    assert ed.text() == "x" * n            # buffer is intact, not torn
    assert len(ed._undo_stack) == n        # one recorded command per edit
    assert len(ed._redo_stack) == 0        # every edit cleared redo


def test_concurrent_undo_after_inserts_is_consistent():
    """After N inserts, N undos from many threads must drain to empty exactly."""
    n = 30
    ed = ThreadSafeTextEditor()
    for _ in range(n):
        ed.insert(0, "x")
    assert len(ed.text()) == n

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def undo_one() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        ed.undo()

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=undo_one) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert ed.text() == ""                 # all undone, no double-pop crash
    assert len(ed._undo_stack) == 0
    assert len(ed._redo_stack) == n

All tests pass. Here's the complete, interview-ready design.

## вП±пЄП 60-Minute Interview Plan

**Interview mode: B вАФ coding environment. I write and RUN the code; a 7-case demo and a 27-test suite prove it.** (In a whiteboard shop, skip ¬І7's run and narrate the dry-runs in ¬І5.5 / ¬І6 instead.)

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0вАУ5 min | **Say ¬І1-2 out loud** вАФ restate "an editor with undo/redo", propose scope (insert/delete/replace, full undo+redo, reject bad positions, single-user). | рЯЧ£пЄП SAY |
| 5вАУ8 min | **Pin the API surface (¬І3.5)** вАФ state the 6 public calls: `insert`, `delete`, `replace`, `undo`, `redo`, `text`. "Before I design classes, here's the contract callers use." | рЯЧ£пЄП SAY |
| 8вАУ14 min | **Draw the 5 boxes** вАФ `TextEditor`, `Document`, `EditCommand` + its concretes, `Memento`. Mention `ThreadSafeTextEditor` verbally. | вЬНпЄП DRAW |
| 14вАУ16 min | **State patterns + the concurrency flag** вАФ Command (every edit is a reversible object), Memento (snapshot fallback); then the honest note: "this is a single-user domain, so concurrency isn't the focus вАФ but I'll show the one-lock story at the end." | рЯЧ£пЄП SAY |
| 16вАУ18 min | **Walk ¬І5.5** вАФ the key insight: carry the inverse, don't snapshot every keystroke; explain `insert` and `undo` in plain words. | рЯЧ£пЄП SAY |
| 18вАУ38 min | **Write code in order** (¬І6.0): exceptions вЖТ `Document` вЖТ `Memento` вЖТ `EditCommand` + concretes вЖТ `TextEditor` skeleton вЖТ `insert` вЖТ `undo`/`redo`. | вЬНпЄП WRITE |
| 38вАУ45 min | **Run `main.py`** вАФ show build-up, undo/redo exactness, fresh-edit-clears-redo, typed errors, the Memento fallback, and the concurrency case. | run |
| 45вАУ55 min | **Concurrency answer** вАФ name what is and isn't a race here, the one-editor-lock fix via the `_lock` hook, and why real collaborative editing is a different problem. | рЯЧ£пЄП SAY |
| 55вАУ60 min | **Follow-ups** вАФ bounded history, grouping keystrokes into one undo, OT/CRDT for multi-user. | вЭУ IF ASKED |

**The "strong hire" moment:** don't fake an elaborate concurrency model for a single-user editor. Say it plainly вАФ "the interesting engineering here is reversible operations, not locking" вАФ then nail the one-lock consistency story and park collaborative editing as an extensibility direction. Honesty about where the difficulty actually lives reads as senior.

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

We're building the *core* of a text editor вАФ the part that holds the text and lets you change it, with unlimited undo and redo. You can type characters in at a spot, delete a stretch, or swap one stretch for another; and at any point you can walk backward through your edits and forward again. Think of the engine behind the edit buffer in VS Code or Google Docs, minus the UI, files, and the collaboration. The whole point of the exercise is clean object modelling of *reversible operations* вАФ how do you make "undo" exact and cheap?

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "Let me lock the operation set first. I'll support three edits вАФ insert text at a position, delete a range, and replace a range вАФ plus `undo`, `redo`, and a way to read the current text. That's the v1 surface, right?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's exactly it."
*вЬУ scoped: three edits + undo + redo + read. Everything else is a follow-up.*

**You:** "For positions I'll use simple integer offsets into a flat character buffer вАФ position 0 is before the first char, position len is after the last. Multi-cursor, line/column coordinates, and rich text are out of scope. Good?"
**Interviewer:** "Good, integer offsets are fine."
*вЬУ flat buffer, integer offsets вАФ I can revisit a rope/gap-buffer for huge files in ¬І9.*

**You:** "Undo has to restore the **exact** previous state. I'll take that to mean the buffer text and the caret position both come back. And redo reapplies an undone edit вАФ but the moment you make a *fresh* edit after undoing, the redo history is thrown away. That's the standard editor behavior вАФ agreed?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed вАФ fresh edit clears redo, that's important."
*вЬУ the redo-fork rule is a first-class requirement, not a detail.*

**You:** "Out-of-range edits вАФ inserting at position 99 in a 3-char doc, deleting past the end вАФ I'll reject with a typed error *before* mutating anything, and I'll report nothing-to-undo and nothing-to-redo as their own clear errors rather than silent no-ops. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes, typed errors, no silent failures."
*вЬУ validate at the boundary; every call has a defined outcome.*

**You:** "On history size вАФ I'll assume unbounded undo for v1, and the additional requirement says each undo step should be O(1), so I'll **carry the inverse of each edit** rather than snapshotting the whole document per keystroke. I'll keep a Memento snapshot path as a fallback for edits that can't be cheaply inverted. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "That's the heart of it вАФ yes."
*вЬУ inverse-command is the headline; Memento is the documented fallback.*

**You:** "Last one вАФ this is a single-user editor, so I'll treat it as essentially single-threaded. I'll keep the core lock-free and clean, and add a modest thread-safe wrapper that serializes operations with one lock, in case the buffer is touched from a UI thread plus a background thread. Real multi-user collaborative editing (operational transform / CRDT) I'll call out as an extensibility direction, not the core. Fair?"
**Interviewer:** "Fair вАФ don't over-engineer the concurrency."
*вЬУ honest scoping: one lock for consistency, OT/CRDT parked for ¬І9.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Insert** вАФ `insert(pos, text)` splices `text` into the buffer at `pos`.
   - Returns nothing on success; raises `InvalidPositionError` if `pos` is outside `[0, len]`.
2. **Delete** вАФ `delete(pos, length)` removes `length` characters starting at `pos`.
   - Raises `InvalidPositionError` if the span runs outside the buffer or `length < 0`.
3. **Replace** вАФ `replace(pos, length, text)` removes a range and inserts new text in one step (one undo unit).
   - Same validation as delete; equivalent in result to a delete then an insert at `pos`.
4. **Undo** вАФ `undo()` reverses the most recent edit, restoring the exact previous text **and caret**.
   - Raises `NothingToUndoError` when there is no edit to undo.
5. **Redo** вАФ `redo()` reapplies the most recently undone edit.
   - Raises `NothingToRedoError` when there is nothing to redo.
   - A fresh edit (1вАУ3) after an undo **clears** the redo history.
6. **Read** вАФ `text()` returns the current buffer contents.
7. **Performance** вАФ each undo/redo step is one command pop+apply (no full-document snapshot per keystroke); history is unbounded by default.
8. **Consistency under threads** вАФ if the editor is touched from multiple threads, an edit either fully happens or not at all; the buffer and the two stacks never desynchronise.

**Out of scope**
- Files / persistence / loading from disk (we operate on an in-memory buffer).
- Line/column coordinates, rich text, syntax highlighting, multi-cursor (UI concerns above the buffer).
- Real-time collaborative editing вАФ OT / CRDT (a distributed problem; parked as an extensibility twist in ¬І9).
- Bounded/compacted history and keystroke-coalescing (noted as follow-ups in ¬І9).

> рЯТђ **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything I want to lock the one phrase that matters вАФ 'restore the exact previous state.' I'll read that as text-plus-caret, and I'll achieve it by making each edit carry its own inverse rather than snapshotting the whole document. With that, let me walk through the actors and flows."

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **User / UI** вАФ the only initiator. Calls `insert` / `delete` / `replace` to edit, `undo` / `redo` to time-travel, `text` to render. (In a real app this is the view layer reacting to keystrokes and Ctrl-Z / Ctrl-Y.)
- **Plugin / macro (optional)** вАФ a programmatic caller that may run an opaque whole-buffer transform; it's why the Memento fallback exists. Not in the v1 public surface, but the seam is there.

There are no system actors (no timers, no background sweeps) and no external actors (no payment/notification systems). This is a self-contained in-memory engine вАФ which is exactly why concurrency isn't the star here.

**Core flows**
1. Edit: User вЖТ `insert/delete/replace` вЖТ editor builds a Command вЖТ command edits the Document вЖТ command pushed on undo stack, redo cleared.
2. Undo: User вЖТ `undo` вЖТ pop undo stack вЖТ command reverses itself on the Document вЖТ command pushed on redo stack.
3. Redo: User вЖТ `redo` вЖТ pop redo stack вЖТ command re-executes on the Document вЖТ command pushed back on undo stack.
4. Read: User вЖТ `text` вЖТ editor returns the Document's current buffer.

## 3. Core Entities (accept / reject)

### 3.1 Noun walkthrough (accept / reject one by one)

> **Document** вАФ The buffer of characters plus the caret. It owns the only mutable state in the system and enforces the one rule that matters (every position must land inside the buffer). Has data *and* behavior (splice in, cut out). **Accepted.**
>
> **TextEditor** вАФ The thing the user actually talks to. It owns the Document and the two history stacks and decides when redo gets cleared. Data + behavior + it's the coordination point. **Accepted (the orchestrator).**
>
> **EditCommand** вАФ An edit modelled as an object that can run itself forward (`execute`) and backward (`undo`). This is the load-bearing abstraction вАФ it's *why* undo is cheap and *why* the editor has zero "if it was an insert, delete it" branches. **Accepted (as an interface with concretes).**
>
> **Insert / Delete / Replace** вАФ Each is a distinct kind of edit with its own inverse logic. They're the concrete `EditCommand`s. **Accepted (one class each).**
>
> **Memento** вАФ A frozen snapshot of the buffer + caret. It has its own identity and a clear job (be an exact, immutable photo to restore from). It's the fallback undo mechanism for edits with no cheap inverse. **Accepted (a frozen value object).**
>
> **UndoStack / RedoStack** вАФ Tempting to model as classes, but they're just last-in-first-out lists of `EditCommand`. No behavior beyond push/pop, no invariant of their own. *Rejected as entities вАФ they're two `list[EditCommand]` fields on `TextEditor`.*
>
> **Position / Length** вАФ A position is just an integer offset; a length is just an integer. No state, no behavior. *Rejected вАФ plain `int` parameters validated by the Document.*
>
> **Cursor / Caret** вАФ Real, but it's a single integer that lives *on* the Document (it's part of the Document's state). It doesn't need its own class. *Rejected as a standalone entity вАФ it's the `_cursor: int` field on Document.*
>
> **Character / Text** вАФ The content itself is a Python `str`. *Rejected вАФ a primitive, the Document's `_text` field.*
>
> **History / Timeline** вАФ A nice conceptual word, but "the history" *is* the pair of stacks plus the clear-on-fresh-edit rule, all owned by the editor. *Rejected вАФ emergent from the two stacks, not its own object.*

### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TextEditor` | Coordinate the document + the two history stacks; decide when redo is cleared | `_document: Document`, `_undo_stack: list[EditCommand]`, `_redo_stack: list[EditCommand]` | A fresh edit always clears redo; undo/redo move exactly one command between stacks |
| `Document` | Own the buffer + caret and be the single validated door to mutate them | `_text: str`, `_cursor: int` | Every position touched lies in `[0, len]`; a span never runs past the end |
| `EditCommand` (abstract) | Define the reversible-operation contract | вАФ | Every concrete edit can `execute` and `undo` itself exactly |
| `InsertCommand` | Insert text; reverse by deleting it back out | `_pos: int`, `_text: str`, `_cursor_before: int` | `undo` removes exactly what `execute` inserted |
| `DeleteCommand` | Delete a span; reverse by re-inserting the removed substring | `_pos: int`, `_length: int`, `_removed: str` | `undo` restores the exact deleted text |
| `ReplaceCommand` | Swap a span for new text in one undo unit | `_pos`, `_length`, `_text`, `_removed: str` | `undo` reverses insert-then-delete in the right order |
| `SnapshotCommand` | Memento fallback for an opaque transform | `_transform`, `_before: Memento`, `_after: Memento` | `undo`/`redo` restore the correct frozen snapshot |
| `Memento` | Be an immutable photo of the whole document | `text: str`, `cursor: int` (frozen) | Once taken, it can never change вАФ restore is exact |

**Enums:** none. This domain has no finite status sets вАФ an edit is an *object*, not a state value, and the Document has no lifecycle states beyond its text/caret. (Saying "no enums needed here" is itself a small signal that you don't reach for machinery you don't need.)

### 3.3 рЯОЩпЄП Spoken intro

> "So the model is small and sharp. `TextEditor` is the brain вАФ it owns the one `Document` and two stacks of commands, and it's the only thing that knows the rule 'a new edit kills the redo future.' `Document` is the single piece of mutable state: a string and a caret, behind two tiny primitives. The interesting abstraction is `EditCommand` вАФ every edit is an object that runs itself forward and backward, so the editor never branches on edit type; `Insert`, `Delete`, and `Replace` each carry a cheap inverse, and `SnapshotCommand` is the Memento fallback for edits we can't cheaply reverse. The things I deliberately left OUT are the undo/redo *stacks* вАФ they're just two lists вАФ and position, length, cursor, and the text itself вАФ those are integers and a string, not classes. Modelling them as entities would be ceremony with no payoff."

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

This is the contract external code calls вАФ the orchestrator's **public methods only**. Every method traces back to a ¬І1.3 requirement; everything else (commands running themselves, the document splicing text, snapshots being taken) is internal.

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `insert` | `(pos: int, text: str) -> None` | вАФ | `InvalidPositionError` | User types text in at a position (FR1) |
| `delete` | `(pos: int, length: int) -> None` | вАФ | `InvalidPositionError` | User removes a range of characters (FR2) |
| `replace` | `(pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None` | вАФ | `InvalidPositionError` | User swaps a range for new text in one undo step (FR3) |
| `undo` | `() -> None` | вАФ | `NothingToUndoError` | Reverse the last edit, restoring exact prior state (FR4) |
| `redo` | `() -> None` | вАФ | `NothingToRedoError` | Reapply the last undone edit (FR5) |
| `text` | `() -> str` | the current buffer contents | вАФ | Read the document for rendering (FR6) |

> рЯОЩпЄП "So the entire editor is six calls: three that change the buffer, two that move through history, and one to read it. Notice every mutating call returns `None` вАФ the user's feedback is the buffer changing, which they read back with `text()`. There's no `get_history` or `can_undo` in the surface; the failure modes are typed exceptions instead. If you were handed just this table you'd know exactly how to drive the editor without seeing a single class."

**Grounding:** every row above appears unchanged as a public method on `TextEditor` in ¬І4.1, in the ¬І4.2 diagram, and in the ¬І6 code. The editor has **no** public method outside this table. (`ThreadSafeTextEditor` adds no new public methods вАФ it only overrides the internal `_lock` hook.)

## 4. Class Design (derive each class, then the diagram)

### 4.1.1 `TextEditor` (the orchestrator)

**(a) Role** вАФ the system's public API. External code calls it to edit and to time-travel; it owns the Document and the two history stacks and is the single place that knows the redo-clearing rule.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "edit the buffer" (FR1вАУ3) | the one `Document` |
| "undo the last edit" (FR4) | a stack of executed commands вАФ the undo stack |
| "redo the last undone edit" (FR5) | a stack of undone commands вАФ the redo stack |

```
class TextEditor:
    - _document: Document
    - _undo_stack: list[EditCommand]
    - _redo_stack: list[EditCommand]
```

*State placement trade-off:* the caret and buffer are **physical** state of the Document, so they live there. The two stacks are **relational/system** state вАФ they describe the editor's managed history, not the document's content вАФ so they live on the orchestrator. (Reload a Document from a Memento and the history is unaffected; that's the tell they belong apart.)

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| insert / delete / replace (FR1вАУ3) | `insert(pos, text)`, `delete(pos, length)`, `replace(pos, length, text)` |
| undo / redo (FR4вАУ5) | `undo()`, `redo()` |
| read (FR6) | `text()` |
| "a fresh edit clears redo" (FR5) | private `_record(command)` |
| concurrency hook (FR8) | private `_lock()` (no-op here, overridden in the subclass) |

```
class TextEditor:
    - _document: Document
    - _undo_stack: list[EditCommand]
    - _redo_stack: list[EditCommand]

    + TextEditor(text="")
    + insert(pos, text) -> None
    + delete(pos, length) -> None
    + replace(pos, length, text) -> None
    + undo() -> None
    + redo() -> None
    + text() -> str
    - _record(command) -> None
    - _lock() -> contextmanager
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why do the mutating calls return `None`?** The user's signal is the buffer changing; they read it back with `text()`. Returning the new text on every keystroke would couple the engine to a rendering decision it shouldn't own.
- **Why `execute` *before* `_record`?** If the position is bad, `execute` raises and we never touch the stacks вАФ a rejected edit leaves history exactly as it was. Validate-then-record, never record-then-validate.
- **Why is `_record` the only place redo is cleared?** One rule, one location. If three methods each cleared redo, one would eventually forget вАФ by funnelling all edits through `_record`, the fork-the-timeline rule is impossible to get wrong.

### 4.1.2 `Document` (the buffer + caret)

**(a) Role** вАФ the single source of mutable truth. It owns the raw text and caret and is the *only* door through which they change, so range-checking lives in exactly one place.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "splice / cut text" (FR1вАУ3) | the buffer `_text: str` |
| "restore the exact caret" (FR4) | the caret `_cursor: int` |

```
class Document:
    - _text: str
    - _cursor: int
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| commands splice text in | `_apply_insert(pos, text)` |
| commands cut text out and learn what was removed | `_apply_delete(pos, length) -> str` |
| undo restores the exact caret | `_move_cursor(pos)` |
| Memento fallback snapshots / restores | `snapshot() -> Memento`, `restore(memento)` |
| read + validation | `text`, `cursor`, `_validate_position(pos, length)` |

```
class Document:
    - _text: str
    - _cursor: int

    + Document(text="")
    + text -> str            (property)
    + cursor -> int          (property)
    + __len__() -> int
    - _validate_position(pos, length=0) -> None
    - _apply_insert(pos, text) -> None
    - _apply_delete(pos, length) -> str
    - _move_cursor(pos) -> None
    + snapshot() -> Memento
    + restore(memento) -> None
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why does `_apply_delete` *return* the removed substring?** That return value is what lets `DeleteCommand` carry its own cheap inverse вАФ undo just re-inserts this exact string. The Document hands the inverse info to the command at the moment of deletion.
- **Why are `_apply_insert` / `_apply_delete` underscore-private?** They're primitives only the Command classes drive. Keeping them low-level (no history awareness) means the Document knows nothing about undo вАФ it just edits text. Single responsibility.
- **Why validate inside the Document, not the editor?** One door, one guard. Every mutation path вАФ even the Memento restore вАФ funnels through here, so an out-of-range edit can't slip in via a second route.

### 4.1.3 `Memento` (the snapshot value object)

**(a) Role** вАФ an immutable photograph of the whole document (text + caret) at one instant. It's the fallback undo mechanism: snapshot before an opaque edit, restore on undo.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "restore exact previous state" for non-invertible edits (FR4/FR7) | the full text and the caret at snapshot time |

```
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Memento:
    text: str
    cursor: int
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| be a read-only carrier of state | none вАФ it's a frozen data holder; `Document.snapshot/restore` do the work |

```
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Memento:
    + text: str
    + cursor: int
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why frozen?** A snapshot that could mutate after capture would be a lie вАФ restoring it might not reproduce the past. `frozen=True` makes Python enforce immutability, so "restore is exact" is guaranteed by construction.
- **Why does the Memento have no methods?** Encapsulation belongs with the originator: `Document.snapshot()` creates it and `Document.restore()` consumes it. The Memento stays a dumb, safe carrier вАФ that's the classic GoF split (originator / memento / caretaker).

### 4.1.4 `EditCommand` (the Command interface)

**(a) Role** вАФ the reversible-operation contract. The editor's stacks hold `EditCommand`s and never know which concrete edit they're reversing вАФ that's the seam that makes new edit types additive.

**(b) State derivation** вАФ none; an interface has no state.

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| (interface only) | nothing вАФ concretes hold their own inverse info |

```
abstract class EditCommand:
    (no fields)
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| apply / reapply an edit (FR1вАУ3, FR5) | `execute(doc)` |
| reverse an edit (FR4) | `undo(doc)` |

```
abstract class EditCommand:
    + execute(doc: Document) -> None    (abstract)
    + undo(doc: Document) -> None       (abstract)
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why is `execute` reused for redo?** Redo *is* re-doing the original edit вАФ the same forward operation. Giving commands one `execute` keeps the editor's redo path to a single line and guarantees redo reproduces exactly what the first edit did.
- **Why an ABC instead of duck typing?** The `@abstractmethod`s document the contract and make "I forgot to implement undo" a construction-time error, not a runtime surprise three months later.

### 4.1.5 `InsertCommand`

**(a) Role** вАФ represent "insert `text` at `pos`" as a reversible object. Inverse: delete the same span back out.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| reverse an insert exactly (FR4) | where (`_pos`), what (`_text`), and the caret before (`_cursor_before`) |

```
class InsertCommand:
    - _pos: int
    - _text: str
    - _cursor_before: int
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| apply the insert (FR1) | `execute(doc)` |
| reverse it (FR4) | `undo(doc)` |

```
class InsertCommand(EditCommand):
    - _pos: int
    - _text: str
    - _cursor_before: int

    + InsertCommand(pos, text)
    + execute(doc) -> None     # capture caret, _apply_insert(pos, text)
    + undo(doc) -> None        # _apply_delete(pos, len(text)); _move_cursor(before)
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why store `_cursor_before`?** "Exact previous state" includes the caret. The inverse of the *text* is computable (delete what we inserted), but the caret isn't вАФ so we capture it. One int, and undo is literally exact.
- **Why no document reference in the constructor?** The command is told which document to act on at `execute`/`undo` time. That keeps a command reusable and the editor in control of *which* document вАФ important if we ever supported multiple buffers.

### 4.1.6 `DeleteCommand`

**(a) Role** вАФ represent "delete `length` chars at `pos`" as a reversible object. Inverse: re-insert the removed substring.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| reverse a delete exactly (FR4) | where (`_pos`), how much (`_length`), the removed text (`_removed`), caret before |

```
class DeleteCommand:
    - _pos: int
    - _length: int
    - _removed: str
    - _cursor_before: int
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| apply the delete (FR2) | `execute(doc)` вАФ captures `_removed` from `_apply_delete` |
| reverse it (FR4) | `undo(doc)` вАФ re-inserts `_removed` |

```
class DeleteCommand(EditCommand):
    - _pos: int
    - _length: int
    - _removed: str
    - _cursor_before: int

    + DeleteCommand(pos, length)
    + execute(doc) -> None     # _removed = _apply_delete(pos, length)
    + undo(doc) -> None        # _apply_insert(pos, _removed); _move_cursor(before)
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why capture `_removed` on execute, not in the constructor?** The text to remove isn't known until we read it off the live buffer at execute time. Capturing it there means the command holds the *actual* deleted characters вАФ the only thing that makes undo exact.
- **Why is this O(deleted-size) memory, not O(document-size)?** We keep only the cut-out substring, not a copy of the whole buffer. That's the headline win over snapshot-everything.

### 4.1.7 `ReplaceCommand`

**(a) Role** вАФ represent "swap `length` chars at `pos` for `text`" as **one** undo unit (not two). A delete + an insert under a single command.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| reverse a replace exactly (FR4) | `_pos`, `_length`, the new `_text`, the removed `_removed`, caret before |

```
class ReplaceCommand:
    - _pos: int
    - _length: int
    - _text: str
    - _removed: str
    - _cursor_before: int
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| apply the replace (FR3) | `execute(doc)` вАФ delete then insert |
| reverse it (FR4) | `undo(doc)` вАФ reverse the two steps in opposite order |

```
class ReplaceCommand(EditCommand):
    - _pos: int
    - _length: int
    - _text: str
    - _removed: str
    - _cursor_before: int

    + ReplaceCommand(pos, length, text)
    + execute(doc) -> None     # _removed = _apply_delete(pos, length); _apply_insert(pos, text)
    + undo(doc) -> None        # _apply_delete(pos, len(text)); _apply_insert(pos, _removed); _move_cursor(before)
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why one command for replace instead of pushing a delete and an insert?** So one Ctrl-Z undoes the whole replace, not half of it. Coalescing the two primitives into a single command is what makes "replace" a single undo unit вАФ the requirement in FR3.
- **Why reverse the two steps in *opposite* order on undo?** The positions only line up if you undo last-in-first-out: pull the inserted text back out first, then restore the deleted text at the same spot. Do it the other way and the offsets drift.

### 4.1.8 `SnapshotCommand` (the Memento fallback)

**(a) Role** вАФ the reversible wrapper for an edit with **no cheap inverse** (an opaque whole-buffer transform supplied by a plugin/macro). It rides the same stacks as the other commands but reverses itself via Memento, not via a carried inverse.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| reverse a non-invertible edit (FR4, FR7-fallback) | the transform, a `_before` Memento, an `_after` Memento |

```
class SnapshotCommand:
    - _transform: Callable[[str], str]
    - _before: Memento | None
    - _after: Memento | None
```

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| apply / reapply the transform (FR3-like, FR5) | `execute(doc)` вАФ first run computes + caches both snapshots; redo restores `_after` |
| reverse it (FR4) | `undo(doc)` вАФ restore `_before` |

```
class SnapshotCommand(EditCommand):
    - _transform: Callable[[str], str]
    - _before: Memento | None
    - _after: Memento | None

    + SnapshotCommand(transform)
    + execute(doc) -> None     # cache before/after on first run; doc.restore(after)
    + undo(doc) -> None        # doc.restore(before)
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why cache `_after` instead of re-running the transform on redo?** A redo just restores the cached result, so the transform doesn't need to be repeatable (it could read a clock or be expensive). Snapshot once, replay the photo.
- **Why does this cost more than the cheap commands?** It stores two whole-document snapshots вАФ O(document-size). That's exactly the trade-off we *avoid* for keystrokes by carrying inverses; we pay it only for the rare opaque transform where no inverse exists.

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundation)

I let each class own the state and behavior closest to its data. `Document` owns the buffer and caret because it's the only thing that *has* the text вАФ so range-checking and splicing live there. Each `EditCommand` owns the knowledge of how to reverse *itself*, because the command is the only thing that knows what it did (a delete knows the substring it removed; an insert knows the span it added). `TextEditor` owns the two stacks and the redo-clearing rule because it's the only thing that sees the *sequence* of edits. No class reaches into another's state: the editor never sets `_text` directly, and a command never manages history. That single discipline вАФ "who has the data, owns the logic" вАФ is what makes the patterns below fall out naturally rather than being bolted on.

### 5.2 Applied patterns (each earned)

**1. Command вАФ the load-bearing pattern.**
- **Why it fits here:** undo/redo is *the* requirement, and the clean way to make an operation reversible is to model it as an object that carries both directions. The alternative вАФ a giant `if edit_type == INSERT: ...` in the editor's undo вАФ is exactly what we avoid.
- **Interface + concretes:** `EditCommand.execute(doc)` / `undo(doc)` вЖТ `InsertCommand`, `DeleteCommand`, `ReplaceCommand`, `SnapshotCommand`.
- рЯОЩпЄП "Every edit is a little object that runs itself forward and backward. Undo is pop-a-command-and-call-undo; redo is pop-and-call-execute. The editor has zero branching on edit type."
- рЯТђ **Layman gloss:** "It's like every action comes with its own 'undo receipt' attached вАФ to reverse it, I don't think about what it was, I just hand it back its receipt."

**2. Memento вАФ the snapshot fallback (and the teaching contrast).**
- **Why it fits here:** most edits carry a cheap inverse, but some (an opaque whole-buffer transform) can't. For those, the only safe way to reverse is to keep a before-photo. `Memento` is that photo; `SnapshotCommand` is the command that uses it, on the same stacks.
- **Interface + concretes:** `Document.snapshot() -> Memento` / `restore(memento)`, used by `SnapshotCommand`.
- рЯОЩпЄП "When I can't carry an inverse, I fall back to the classic Memento вАФ snapshot the document before, restore it on undo. It costs whole-document memory, so it's the exception, not the rule."
- рЯТђ **Layman gloss:** "For an edit too messy to reverse step-by-step, I just keep a 'save game' from right before it and reload that to undo."

**3. Template Method вАФ the concurrency hook.** (a small, real one)
- **Why it fits here:** I want the clean core to have zero lock code, and the thread-safe version to differ by a few lines. `_lock()` is a no-op context manager the subclass overrides.
- **Interface + concrete:** `TextEditor._lock()` (yields nothing) вЖТ `ThreadSafeTextEditor._lock()` (acquires an `RLock`).
- рЯОЩпЄП "The clean editor's `_lock` does nothing. The thread-safe subclass overrides that one method. The diff between unsafe and safe is about five lines."
- рЯТђ **Layman gloss:** "I leave a labelled slot that says 'a lock could go here'; the safe version fills the slot, the simple version leaves it empty."

### 5.3 SOLID (tied to classes)
- **Single Responsibility:** `Document` owns text mutation, each `EditCommand` owns one reversible edit, `TextEditor` owns coordination + history. Three reasons to change, three classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new edit kind (e.g. `IndentCommand`) is a new `EditCommand` subclass; `TextEditor` doesn't change a line. The `EditCommand` seam is the proof.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeTextEditor` is a drop-in `TextEditor` вАФ same surface, same outcomes, just serialized. Any code holding a `TextEditor` works unchanged.
- **Dependency Inversion:** `TextEditor` depends on the `EditCommand` abstraction and on the `Document` primitives, never on a concrete edit's internals.

- **Memento** is realized: `Memento` is a real frozen dataclass, `Document.snapshot/restore` are real, and `SnapshotCommand` exercises them (proved by `test_snapshot_command_undo_redo`).
- **Template Method** is realized: `_lock` is a real no-op overridden by `ThreadSafeTextEditor` (proved by the concurrency tests running on the subclass).

I did **not** claim Singleton: nothing enforces one editor. I did **not** claim Observer: there are no listeners in v1 (I note where one would attach in ¬І9).

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The key insight

> **Insight:** Model every edit as a Command that *carries its own inverse*, so undo/redo is just two stacks вАФ one command pop+apply per step, unbounded history, and no full-document snapshot per keystroke. Keep Memento as a fallback only for edits with no cheap inverse.

**Why it works:** the three edits are *local* and *exactly invertible* from a tiny amount of captured info вАФ an insert's inverse is "delete what I added" (need only the span), a delete's inverse is "re-insert what I removed" (need only the removed substring), a replace is the two composed. So each command stores O(edit-size) bytes, not O(document-size). Reversing is then mechanical: the editor pops a command and calls `undo`; it never reasons about *what kind* of edit it was.

**What breaks without it:** the naive version snapshots the entire buffer on every keystroke and stores those snapshots for undo. On a 1 MB document, typing 10,000 characters costs ~10 GB of history вАФ the editor OOMs on a normal editing session. Or the engineer skips snapshots and inlines reverse-logic in the editor (`if last_op == "insert": buffer = buffer[:pos] + buffer[pos+n:]`), and within a release someone adds `replace` and forgets to special-case its two-step undo вАФ now Ctrl-Z half-reverses a replace and the buffer is corrupted. Carrying the inverse *in the command* makes both failures impossible by construction: memory is proportional to edit size, and reverse-logic lives with the edit that owns it.

### B. Per-operation algorithm (pseudocode + рЯОЩпЄП scripts)

**`insert(pos, text)` вАФ the edit pipeline:**
```
1. (under the editor lock)
2. build InsertCommand(pos, text)
3. command.execute(document):
4.     capture cursor_before
5.     document._apply_insert(pos, text)   # validates; raises InvalidPositionError if pos bad
6. record(command):                        # only reached if step 5 did not raise
7.     push command on undo stack
8.     clear redo stack
```
> рЯОЩпЄП "Insert is build-execute-record. The whole trick is the order: I execute *before* I record, so a bad position throws in step 5 and we never touch the stacks. Step 8 is the editor-specific rule вАФ a fresh edit clears redo, so you can't redo into a timeline you just abandoned."

**`delete(pos, length)` / `replace(pos, length, text)`** вАФ identical shape; only the command class differs (`DeleteCommand` captures the removed substring in execute; `ReplaceCommand` does delete-then-insert in execute).
```
1. build the command (Delete or Replace)
2. command.execute(document)   # captures inverse info; validates
3. record(command)             # push undo, clear redo
```
> рЯОЩпЄП "Same pipeline as insert. The only difference is which command вАФ and the command, not the editor, knows how to reverse itself. That's why these three methods are three lines each."

**`undo()` вАФ pop, reverse, park:**
```
1. (under the editor lock)
2. if undo stack empty -> raise NothingToUndoError
3. command = undo stack.pop()
4. command.undo(document)       # restores text AND caret
5. redo stack.push(command)
```
> рЯОЩпЄП "Undo is three guards and a move: empty-check (step 2, a typed error not a silent no-op), pop, reverse, and park on the redo stack so redo can replay it. Notice the editor doesn't know *how* to reverse вАФ step 4 delegates to the command."

**`redo()` вАФ the mirror of undo:**
```
1. (under the editor lock)
2. if redo stack empty -> raise NothingToRedoError
3. command = redo stack.pop()
4. command.execute(document)    # re-applies the original edit
5. undo stack.push(command)
```
> рЯОЩпЄП "Redo is undo's mirror вАФ `execute` instead of `undo`, and the command goes back on the undo stack. Re-using `execute` is why redo can't drift from the original edit: it's literally the same forward operation."

**`text()` вАФ read:**
```
1. (under the editor lock) return document.text
```
> рЯОЩпЄП "Pure read. It's under the lock only so a concurrent edit can't be observed half-applied."

### BвА≤. DRY RUN вАФ `insert` then `undo` then `redo` (state-change table)

> **Dry run вАФ start from `Document("Hello")`, caret at 5:**
>
> | Step | Call | `_text` | `_cursor` | undo stack | redo stack |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 0 | initial | `"Hello"` | 5 | `[]` | `[]` |
> | 1 | `insert(5, " World")` | `"Hello World"` | 11 | `[Ins]` | `[]` |
> | 2 | `replace(0, 5, "Hi")` | `"Hi World"` | 2 | `[Ins, Rep]` | `[]` |
> | 3 | `undo()` | `"Hello World"` | 5 | `[Ins]` | `[Rep]` |
> | 4 | `undo()` | `"Hello"` | 5 | `[]` | `[Rep, Ins]` |
> | 5 | `redo()` | `"Hello World"` | 11 | `[Ins]` | `[Rep]` |
> | 6 | `insert(0, "X")` | `"XHello World"` | 1 | `[Ins, Ins2]` | `[]` вЖР **redo cleared** |
>
> *Edge probe: at step 6 the fresh insert clears the redo stack, so the `Rep` we'd undone is gone forever вАФ exactly FR5's fork-the-timeline rule. And note step 3/4 restore the caret to 5 (its value before each edit), not to the edit site вАФ that's the `_cursor_before` capture paying off.*

### C. Data-structure choices

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `list[EditCommand]` (undo stack) | append + pop the most recent edit | a stack is the natural LIFO for "most recent first"; Python list append/pop are amortized O(1) at the tail. A deque would only help if we also popped the *oldest* (we don't, until bounded history вАФ ¬І9) | O(1) push/pop |
| `list[EditCommand]` (redo stack) | hold undone edits; cleared on fresh edit | `list.clear()` is O(1); a separate stack (not a pointer into one history list) makes "clear the future" a single call with no index juggling | O(1) push/pop/clear |
| `str` buffer in `Document` | the text | simplest correct model; slicing is clear and immutable-friendly for Mementos. Trade-off: insert/delete are O(n) because a `str` is contiguous. Fine for interview-scale docs | O(n) per edit, O(1) read |
| per-command captured `str` (`_removed`) / `int` span | the cheap inverse | O(edit-size) memory beats a per-keystroke whole-document `Memento` (O(doc-size)). This is the headline insight, made concrete | O(edit-size) space |
| `Memento(text, cursor)` | snapshot fallback only | O(doc-size) per snapshot вАФ acceptable because it's used only for rare non-invertible transforms, never per keystroke | O(n) space, O(n) restore |

I deliberately chose a flat `str` over a **rope** or **gap buffer**. Threshold to flip: when documents grow past ~a few hundred KB and inserts in the middle become a measurable hot spot, I'd switch the `Document`'s buffer to a rope (O(log n) splice) вАФ and *nothing else changes*, because only the two primitives `_apply_insert`/`_apply_delete` touch the representation. That isolation is the payoff of funnelling all mutation through the Document.

### D. Tricky logic вАФ claim, proof, fix

> **рЯІ† Claim:** `execute` must run and succeed *before* the command is recorded on the undo stack.
> **рЯФђ Proof:** if we pushed first and validated second, an `insert(99, "x")` into a 3-char doc would leave a dead command on the undo stack; a later `undo()` would try to `_apply_delete(99, 1)` and raise from deep inside, corrupting the caret/stack relationship.
> **рЯЫ†пЄП Fix in code:** `TextEditor.insert` calls `command.execute(self._document)` (which validates and may raise) and only then calls `self._record(command)`. A rejected edit never reaches the stack. Covered by `test_rejected_edit_leaves_history_untouched`.

> **рЯІ† Claim:** a fresh edit after an undo must clear the redo stack.
> **рЯФђ Proof:** undo "World"вЖТnothing, then type "X". If redo weren't cleared, pressing redo would try to reapply the abandoned edit on a buffer that no longer matches its assumptions вАФ reapplying an insert at a position that's now out of range, or silently producing nonsense text.
> **рЯЫ†пЄП Fix in code:** every mutating method funnels through `_record`, whose second line is `self._redo_stack.clear()`. One place, impossible to forget. Covered by `test_fresh_edit_after_undo_clears_redo`.

> **рЯІ† Claim:** `ReplaceCommand.undo` must reverse its two steps in the *opposite* order.
> **рЯФђ Proof:** execute does delete-at-`pos` then insert-`text`-at-`pos`. If undo also did "delete the old length then insert the removed" it would delete the wrong characters (the buffer now holds `text`, not the original). The inverse of (A then B) is (B-inverse then A-inverse).
> **рЯЫ†пЄП Fix in code:** `undo` first `_apply_delete(pos, len(text))` (removes what we inserted), then `_apply_insert(pos, removed)` (restores the original). Covered by `test_undo_then_redo_restores_exactly` and `test_insert_and_replace_commands_round_trip`.

> **рЯІ† Claim:** "exact previous state" includes the caret, which is *not* recoverable from the text inverse alone.
> **рЯФђ Proof:** after `insert(3, "ZZZ")` the caret is at 6; the text inverse (delete 3 at 3) would naturally leave the caret at 3, not where it was before the edit. UX-wise, undo should put the caret back where the user was.
> **рЯЫ†пЄП Fix in code:** each command captures `_cursor_before` in `execute` and calls `doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)` at the end of `undo`. Covered by `test_undo_restores_exact_cursor`.

> **рЯІ† Claim:** `SnapshotCommand` redo must not re-run the transform.
> **рЯФђ Proof:** the transform may be non-deterministic or expensive; re-running it on every redo could produce a *different* result than the original edit, breaking redo's "reapply exactly" contract.
> **рЯЫ†пЄП Fix in code:** `execute` caches `_after` on the first run and `doc.restore(self._after)` on every subsequent call. Covered by `test_snapshot_command_undo_redo`.

### E. вЭМ The wrong version a junior writes

> **Naive `undo` that inlines reverse-logic in the editor:**
> ```
> def undo(self):
>     op = self._history.pop()                 # op is a tuple ("insert", pos, text)
>     if op[0] == "insert":
>         self._text = self._text[:op[1]] + self._text[op[1]+len(op[2]):]
>     elif op[0] == "delete":
>         ...                                  # forgot to restore the removed text!
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** two failure modes. (1) The editor now owns reverse-logic for *every* edit type вАФ add `replace` and you must touch this `if/elif`, and the day someone forgets, undo silently corrupts the buffer. (2) The `delete` branch has nowhere to get the removed text from, because the tuple never captured it вАФ so delete simply *can't* be undone. The correct version makes each edit an object that captured its own inverse at execute time, and `undo` is just `command.undo(doc)` вАФ no branching, no missing data.
