### 6.1 Exceptions + `Document` (`models.py`)

рЯОЩпЄП "Vocabulary first вАФ one base error, one subclass per failure mode."

```python
class EditorError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises вАФ callers catch one type."""


class InvalidPositionError(EditorError):
    """A position or length falls outside the buffer (out-of-range edit)."""


class NothingToUndoError(EditorError):
    """undo() was called but the undo stack is empty."""


class NothingToRedoError(EditorError):
    """redo() was called but the redo stack is empty (or a fresh edit cleared it)."""
```

рЯОЩпЄП "Now the Document вАФ the single mutable thing, a buffer and a caret behind two primitives. Validation lives in one place."

```python
class Document:
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        self._text = text
        self._cursor = len(text)              # caret sits between chars; range 0..len

    @property
    def text(self) -> str:
        return self._text

    @property
    def cursor(self) -> int:
        return self._cursor

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self._text)

    def _validate_position(self, pos: int, length: int = 0) -> None:
        if length < 0:
            raise InvalidPositionError(f"length must be >= 0, got {length}")
        if pos < 0 or pos > len(self._text):
            raise InvalidPositionError(f"position {pos} out of range [0, {len(self._text)}]")
        if pos + length > len(self._text):
            raise InvalidPositionError(f"span [{pos}, {pos + length}) runs past end {len(self._text)}")

    def _apply_insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        self._validate_position(pos)
        self._text = self._text[:pos] + text + self._text[pos:]
        self._cursor = pos + len(text)        # caret lands just after typed text

    def _apply_delete(self, pos: int, length: int) -> str:
        self._validate_position(pos, length)
        removed = self._text[pos : pos + length]   # WHY: return it -> the command's cheap inverse
        self._text = self._text[:pos] + self._text[pos + length :]
        self._cursor = pos
        return removed

    def _move_cursor(self, pos: int) -> None:
        self._validate_position(pos)          # undo restores the EXACT caret
        self._cursor = pos

    def snapshot(self) -> Memento:
        return Memento(text=self._text, cursor=self._cursor)

    def restore(self, memento: Memento) -> None:
        self._text = memento.text
        self._cursor = memento.cursor
```

This is the only file that touches the raw buffer. `_apply_delete` returning the removed substring is the quiet hero вАФ that return value *is* the inverse that `DeleteCommand` carries, so undo never needs a snapshot. Notice `_validate_position` is reused by all three mutators *and* by `_move_cursor`, so there is precisely one definition of "in range." We assume the buffer fits in memory and slicing is acceptable; for very large documents I'd swap `str` for a rope behind these same two primitives without touching anything else.

### 6.2 `Memento` (single block вАФ a frozen photo)

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Memento:
    text: str
    cursor: int
```

`frozen=True` is doing real work: once a snapshot is taken it can never drift, so restoring it is guaranteed to reproduce the past. The Document is the originator (it creates and consumes Mementos); the Memento itself stays a dumb, safe carrier вАФ the textbook GoF split.

### 6.3 `EditCommand` + concretes (`strategies.py`)

рЯОЩпЄП "The heart вАФ every edit is an object that runs itself forward and backward."

```python
class EditCommand(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        """Apply this edit (also used to redo it)."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    @abstractmethod
    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        """Reverse this edit, restoring the pre-execute state."""
        raise NotImplementedError
```

```python
class InsertCommand(EditCommand):
    def __init__(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._text = text
        self._cursor_before = 0               # captured on execute -> exact caret on undo

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._text)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        doc._apply_delete(self._pos, len(self._text))   # inverse of insert = delete same span
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)
```

```python
class DeleteCommand(EditCommand):
    def __init__(self, pos: int, length: int) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._length = length
        self._removed = ""                    # the inverse info, captured on execute
        self._cursor_before = 0

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        self._removed = doc._apply_delete(self._pos, self._length)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed)
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)
```

```python
class ReplaceCommand(EditCommand):
    def __init__(self, pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._length = length
        self._text = text
        self._removed = ""
        self._cursor_before = 0

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        self._removed = doc._apply_delete(self._pos, self._length)   # delete first ...
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._text)                     # ... then insert

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        doc._apply_delete(self._pos, len(self._text))   # reverse order: pull inserted text out
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed)     # then restore the old text
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)
```

```python
class SnapshotCommand(EditCommand):
    def __init__(self, transform: Callable[[str], str]) -> None:
        self._transform = transform
        self._before: Memento | None = None
        self._after: Memento | None = None

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        if self._after is None:               # first run: compute + cache both snapshots
            self._before = doc.snapshot()
            new_text = self._transform(doc.text)
            self._after = Memento(text=new_text, cursor=len(new_text))
        doc.restore(self._after)              # redo just restores the cached result

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        assert self._before is not None       # editor only undoes an executed command
        doc.restore(self._before)
```

The four concretes split cleanly into two families. `Insert`/`Delete`/`Replace` carry a *cheap inverse* вАФ a span length or a removed substring, O(edit-size) вАФ captured at `execute` time off the live buffer. `Snapshot` is the *Memento* family: it can't carry a cheap inverse for an arbitrary transform, so it photographs the document before and after and flips between the photos. Both are `EditCommand`s on the same stacks, so the editor treats them identically вАФ which is the whole point of the Command seam.

### 6.4 `TextEditor` вАФ skeleton first (`editor_service.py`)

рЯОЩпЄП "The brain вАФ one document, two stacks. Here's the whole API in one glance."

```python
class TextEditor:
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        self._document = Document(text)
        self._undo_stack: list[EditCommand] = []
        self._redo_stack: list[EditCommand] = []

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        yield                                  # no-op; ThreadSafeTextEditor overrides

    def insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None: ...
    def delete(self, pos: int, length: int) -> None: ...
    def replace(self, pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None: ...
    def undo(self) -> None: ...
    def redo(self) -> None: ...
    def text(self) -> str: ...
    def _record(self, command: EditCommand) -> None: ...
```

Now the bodies.

#### `insert` вАФ the edit pipeline (6-block)

**1. Role:** turn a user keystroke into a recorded, reversible edit.
**2. Core logic:**
1. build an `InsertCommand(pos, text)`
2. execute it on the document (this validates)
3. record it (push undo, clear redo)
**3. Edge cases:** out-of-range `pos` (execute raises before record); empty `text` (a valid no-content insert).
**4. Code:**
```python
def insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
    with self._lock():
        command = InsertCommand(pos, text)
        command.execute(self._document)        # raises InvalidPositionError before we record
        self._record(command)
```
**5. Reasoning:** this is the build-execute-record pipeline. The single most important line is `command.execute(...)` *above* `self._record(...)`: execute validates and mutates, so if the position is bad it raises here and we never reach `_record` вАФ a rejected edit leaves both stacks untouched. Notice we are **not** validating `pos` in the editor at all; that's the Document's job, reached through the command, so there's exactly one validator. `delete` and `replace` are byte-for-byte the same shape with `DeleteCommand` / `ReplaceCommand` swapped in.
**6.** рЯОЩпЄП Say: "Build, execute, record вАФ and execute before record so a bad position throws before it can dirty the history." вЪ†пЄП "What if `pos` is out of range?" вЖТ "`_apply_insert` raises `InvalidPositionError` inside `execute`, before `_record` runs, so the undo/redo stacks are exactly as they were."

#### `delete` and `replace` вАФ same pipeline

```python
def delete(self, pos: int, length: int) -> None:
    with self._lock():
        command = DeleteCommand(pos, length)
        command.execute(self._document)
        self._record(command)

def replace(self, pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None:
    with self._lock():
        command = ReplaceCommand(pos, length, text)
        command.execute(self._document)
        self._record(command)
```

Three methods, three lines of body each, because the cleverness lives in the command вАФ not here. That uniformity is the Command pattern earning its keep.

#### `undo` / `redo` вАФ move one command between stacks (6-block)

**1. Role:** step backward (or forward) through history by exactly one edit.
**2. Core logic (undo):** empty-check вЖТ pop undo вЖТ `command.undo(doc)` вЖТ push redo.
**3. Edge cases:** empty undo stack вЖТ `NothingToUndoError`; empty redo stack вЖТ `NothingToRedoError`.
**4. Code:**
```python
def undo(self) -> None:
    with self._lock():
        if not self._undo_stack:
            raise NothingToUndoError("there is no edit to undo")
        command = self._undo_stack.pop()
        command.undo(self._document)
        self._redo_stack.append(command)

def redo(self) -> None:
    with self._lock():
        if not self._redo_stack:
            raise NothingToRedoError("there is no edit to redo")
        command = self._redo_stack.pop()
        command.execute(self._document)        # re-applies the original edit
        self._undo_stack.append(command)
```
**5. Reasoning:** `undo` is a guard, a pop, a delegated reverse, and a park. The delegated line вАФ `command.undo(self._document)` вАФ is where the editor stays dumb: it doesn't know or care whether it's reversing an insert, a delete, or a snapshot. `redo` is the exact mirror, and crucially it calls `command.execute`, the *same* forward operation the original edit used, so a redone edit can never drift from the original. The empty-stack cases are typed errors, never silent no-ops, so the caller always knows whether anything happened.
**6.** рЯОЩпЄП Say: "Pop one stack, reverse via the command, push the other. Redo is the mirror вАФ `execute` instead of `undo`." вЪ†пЄП "Why raise instead of returning False on empty?" вЖТ "A typed error is unambiguous and composes with the rest of the API, which is exception-based; a bool would invite callers to ignore it."

#### Helper вАФ `_record`

**Role:** the one place that enforces FR5's fork rule.
```python
def _record(self, command: EditCommand) -> None:
    self._undo_stack.append(command)
    self._redo_stack.clear()                   # a fresh edit kills the redo future
```
Every mutating method funnels through here, so "a new edit clears redo" can never be forgotten вАФ it's a single line in a single method.

### Approach Comparison вАФ how should `ReplaceCommand.undo` work?

This is the one method where the design has a genuine fork in the road: how do you reverse a *compound* edit?

**Approach 1 вАФ snapshot the whole document (rejected for the hot edits):**
```
execute(doc):
    self._before = doc.snapshot()         # photograph the ENTIRE buffer
    doc._apply_delete(pos, length)
    doc._apply_insert(pos, text)
undo(doc):
    doc.restore(self._before)             # reload the photo
```
> **Challenges:** simple and always-correct, but it stores a full copy of the document *per edit*. On a 1 MB file, a thousand replaces is ~1 GB of history. For a keystroke-frequency operation that's a memory disaster вАФ this is exactly the "snapshot everything" anti-pattern the key insight warns against.

**Approach 2 вАФ track a generic forward/backward diff log (rejected for this scale):**
```
maintain a list of (op, pos, payload) diff records;
undo replays the inverse diff records in reverse
```
> **Challenges:** general (handles batched/compound edits), but it puts the reverse-logic in a second place outside the command and needs careful ordering bookkeeping. State now lives in two structures that must stay in sync вАФ forget to append one diff and undo silently corrupts. Worth it only if we needed cross-command grouping (see the "group keystrokes" twist in ¬І9).

**Chosen approach вАФ carry the cheap inverse inside the command:**
```
execute(doc):
    self._removed = doc._apply_delete(pos, length)   # capture only what we removed
    doc._apply_insert(pos, text)
undo(doc):
    doc._apply_delete(pos, len(text))                # reverse order
    doc._apply_insert(pos, self._removed)
```
> **Why it wins:** memory is O(edit-size) вАФ we keep only the removed substring, never the whole buffer вАФ and the reverse-logic lives *with* the edit that owns it, so there's a single source of truth and no second structure to desync. The whole-document `Memento` path still exists (as `SnapshotCommand`) for the genuinely non-invertible case, so we get the cheap path for the 99% and the safe fallback for the 1%.

### Verification вАФ full-lifecycle dry runs

**Scenario 1 вАФ Happy path (`insert вЖТ replace вЖТ undo вЖТ redo`):**
Initial: `Document("Hi")`, cursor 2, `_undo=[]`, `_redo=[]`
```text
1. insert(2, "!")   -> _text="Hi!"   cursor=3   _undo=[Ins]       _redo=[]
2. replace(0,2,"Yo")-> _text="Yo!"   cursor=2   _undo=[Ins,Rep]   _redo=[]
3. undo()           -> _text="Hi!"   cursor=2   _undo=[Ins]       _redo=[Rep]
4. redo()           -> _text="Yo!"   cursor=2   _undo=[Ins,Rep]   _redo=[]
```
Text and both stacks return to their step-2 state after redo. вЬУ

**Scenario 2 вАФ A rejected edit then nothing-to-undo:**
Initial: `Document("abc")`
```text
1. insert(99, "z")  -> raises InvalidPositionError; _text="abc", _undo=[], _redo=[]  (untouched)
2. undo()           -> raises NothingToUndoError    (nothing was recorded)
```
The bad insert never reached `_record`, so history is pristine and `undo` correctly reports there's nothing to reverse. вЬУ

**Scenario 3 вАФ the consistency concern, in plain English (no code):**
Without the editor lock, if a UI thread calls `insert` while a background thread calls `undo` on the *same* editor, the two could interleave: `undo` pops a command and starts reversing it on the Document while `insert` mutates the same buffer вАФ you could observe a half-spliced string, or push to `_undo_stack` while `_redo_stack` is being cleared, leaving the stacks out of sync with the text. With the single `_lock` wrapping each operation, one fully completes before the other begins, so the buffer and both stacks always agree. This is *consistency*, not contention вАФ there's no throughput to win here, just correctness if the object is shared.

### Complexity Summary

| Operation | Time | Space (history delta) | Key reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `insert(pos, text)` | O(n + m) | O(m) | splice into a flat `str` (n = doc size, m = text size); command stores `pos` + length |
| `delete(pos, length)` | O(n) | O(length) | slice rebuild; command stores the removed substring |
| `replace(pos, length, text)` | O(n + m) | O(length + m) | delete + insert; command stores removed substring |
| `undo()` | O(n) | O(1) | pop O(1) + one inverse primitive O(n) on the buffer |
| `redo()` | O(n) | O(1) | pop O(1) + re-execute O(n) on the buffer |
| `text()` | O(1) | O(1) | returns the `str` reference (Python strings are immutable) |

The "per undo step is O(1)" in the requirement refers to the *command* work вАФ one pop and one apply, with O(edit-size) memory and no per-keystroke document snapshot. The O(n) factor is the flat-buffer splice, which a rope would cut to O(log n) behind the same primitives.

## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** I'm showing `insert вЖТ undo вЖТ redo` because it touches every layer вАФ the editor, a command, and the document вАФ and it's where the Command pattern visibly fires twice (once forward via `execute`, once backward via `undo`).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor User
    participant E as TextEditor
    participant C as InsertCommand
    participant D as Document
    User->>E: insert at 6 with World
    E->>C: build InsertCommand
    E->>C: execute on Document
    C->>D: _apply_insert at 6
    D-->>C: text spliced, caret moved
    E->>E: record - push undo, clear redo
    E-->>User: ok
    User->>E: undo
    E->>C: undo on Document
    C->>D: _apply_delete 5 chars at 6
    C->>D: _move_cursor to caret_before
    E->>E: push command to redo
    E-->>User: ok
    User->>E: redo
    E->>C: execute on Document again
    C->>D: _apply_insert at 6
    E->>E: push command to undo
    E-->>User: ok
```

рЯОЩпЄП **Walk through it arrow by arrow:** "The User calls `insert` on the `TextEditor`. The editor doesn't splice text itself вАФ it *builds* an `InsertCommand` and tells it to `execute` on the `Document`. The command calls the Document's `_apply_insert`, which is the only place the buffer changes and the caret moves. Control returns to the editor, which `record`s the command вАФ push onto undo, clear redo. Now the interesting part: on `undo`, the editor pops that same command and calls `command.undo` вАФ the command knows its inverse is `_apply_delete` of the span it added, then it restores the exact caret. The command goes onto the redo stack. On `redo`, the editor calls `command.execute` again вАФ the *same forward operation* вАФ and moves it back to undo. The editor never once branched on 'what kind of edit is this?'."

**Why the sequence shows the design is good:** the `TextEditor` coordinates the flow but implements no edit logic вАФ every mutation delegates to a command, which delegates to the Document's primitives. That's Single Responsibility and Open/Closed at runtime: drop in a new `EditCommand` and this exact sequence works unchanged.

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**Honest framing first:** this is a single-user editor. There is no genuine multi-writer race in the domain вАФ one human edits one buffer. So I am *not* going to invent an elaborate concurrency model. The one real concern is **consistency if the same editor object is touched from more than one thread** (e.g. a UI thread editing while a background thread autosaves or runs a macro). For that, one lock per operation is correct and sufficient.

рЯЧ£пЄП **Step 1 вАФ Name the (modest) hazard:** "An edit mutates three things together вАФ the Document's buffer and the two stacks. If two threads call into the same editor at once, one could observe a half-applied edit: a partially-spliced buffer, or an undo stack that no longer matches the text."

рЯЧ£пЄП **Step 2 вАФ The fix:** "Wrap each whole operation in one editor lock. While a thread holds it, no other thread can enter `insert`/`undo`/etc., so the document and stacks always move together. I do it through the `_lock` hook so the clean core has zero lock code."

рЯЧ£пЄП **Step 3 вАФ The trade-off:** "One lock means operations serialize вАФ but for a single-user editor that's a non-issue; edits are microseconds and human-paced. There's no throughput to recover, so finer-grained locking would be complexity with zero payoff. I keep the lock-acquire out of the clean core and only pay it in the thread-safe subclass."

рЯЧ£пЄП **Step 4 вАФ The distributed / multi-user case:** "If we wanted *real* collaborative editing вАФ many people editing the same document live вАФ a lock is the wrong tool entirely. You can't lock a shared document across clients without killing the experience. That's the domain of **Operational Transform** or **CRDTs**, where concurrent edits are merged rather than serialized. That's a much larger problem and I'd treat it as a separate design, not a bolt-on (see the twist below)."

### Concurrency vocabulary (be fluent)

| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
|---|---|---|
| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once and the result depends on timing | only if a shared editor is hit from 2 threads вАФ not in the single-user domain |
| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the whole body of each public method (edit + both stacks) |
| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing; no half-done state is observable | what the editor lock guarantees per operation |
| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` in `ThreadSafeTextEditor` |
| **Reentrant (RLock)** | The same thread can re-acquire a lock it already holds | used defensively so a future nested op won't self-deadlock |
| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers | coarse here (one editor lock) вАФ correct because there's nothing to parallelize |
| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` approach |
| **Optimistic / CRDT / OT** | Don't lock; merge concurrent changes | the collaborative-editing direction, explicitly out of core scope |
| **Lost update** | One write silently overwrites another | what the concurrency test proves can't happen (length == N) |

> The candidate can say: "It's a single-user domain, so I take a coarse pessimistic lock purely for *consistency* if the object is shared вАФ an `RLock` defensively. There's no contention to optimize, so fine-grained locking would be pointless. Real multi-writer is OT/CRDT, a different problem."

### Concurrency follow-up Q&A

> вЭУ "Why a lock at all if it's single-user?"
> "Defense in depth. A real app often has a background thread вАФ autosave, a macro runner, a spell-check pass вАФ that may touch the buffer. If it does so concurrently with a keystroke, you could tear the buffer or desync the stacks. The lock makes each operation atomic so that's impossible. In a strictly single-threaded embedding you'd just use the lock-free `TextEditor` and pay nothing."

> вЭУ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
> "Honestly, in the current code a plain `Lock` would work вАФ each public method takes the lock once and doesn't call another locked method. I use an `RLock` defensively: if someone later refactors, say, `replace` to call `self.delete` then `self.insert`, a plain `Lock` would self-deadlock on the second acquire, whereas an `RLock` lets the same thread re-enter. It's a cheap insurance against a likely future edit."

> вЭУ "Why one editor lock instead of locking the document and each stack separately?"
> "Because the three must move *together* вАФ the buffer and both stacks are one consistent unit. Separate locks would let a thread see a buffer that's been edited but a stack that hasn't, which is exactly the inconsistency I'm preventing. There's no parallelism to gain by splitting them in a single-user editor, so one lock is both simpler and more correct."

> вЭУ "Is there any deadlock risk?"
> "No. I only ever hold one lock вАФ the editor lock вАФ and I never call out to slow I/O or a second lock while holding it. With a single lock acquired in a single order, there's no cycle, so no deadlock. The `RLock` re-entrancy is the same thread re-taking the same lock, which is safe by definition."

> вЭУ "Where would the lock *hurt*, and would a read-write lock help?"
> "It would only hurt if reads vastly outnumbered writes and reads were expensive вАФ then a read-write lock would let many `text()` calls run in parallel. But `text()` is O(1) (it returns a string reference) and edits mutate, so there's nothing to gain. I'd reach for a read-write lock only if I had a genuinely expensive read-only query path."

### IF ASKED вАФ the clean вЖТ thread-safe diff

**Part A вАФ the no-op hook + the subclass:**
```python
# вФАвФАвФА ¬І6 clean core: the hook does NOTHING, zero lock code in the editor вФАвФАвФА
class TextEditor:
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        yield

# вФАвФАвФА thread-safe: override ONLY this one method вФАвФАвФА
class ThreadSafeTextEditor(TextEditor):
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        super().__init__(text)
        self._mutex = threading.RLock()

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._mutex:
            yield
```
`RLock` (not `Lock`) defensively; one editor-wide lock (not per-resource) because the buffer and both stacks are one consistent unit; no double-lock-the-map idiom is needed because there's a single lock, not a dict of them.

**Part B вАФ the exact modified method.** With the Template-Method hook, `insert` is **identical** in both versions вАФ the only difference is what `_lock()` does:
```python
# вФАвФАвФА ¬І6 version (clean) вАФ _lock() yields, no real lock вФАвФАвФА
def insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
    with self._lock():
        command = InsertCommand(pos, text)
        command.execute(self._document)
        self._record(command)

# вФАвФАвФА ¬І9 version (thread-safe) вАФ SAME method body; _lock() now takes the RLock вФАвФАвФА
def insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
    with self._lock():                         # <- this now acquires self._mutex
        command = InsertCommand(pos, text)
        command.execute(self._document)
        self._record(command)
```
рЯОЩпЄП "The method body doesn't change at all вАФ that's the point of the hook. The clean core has no lock imports; the safe version overrides one method and the same `with self._lock():` line suddenly serializes the operation. The diff to make the whole editor thread-safe is the five-line subclass."

**Part C вАФ prove it with a barrier test:**
```python
def test_concurrent_inserts_under_lock_lose_nothing():
    n = 50
    ed = ThreadSafeTextEditor()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def attempt() -> None:
        barrier.wait()                 # all 50 hit insert at the same instant
        ed.insert(0, "x")

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads: t.start()
    for t in threads: t.join()

    assert len(ed.text()) == n         # every insert landed вАФ no lost update
    assert ed.text() == "x" * n        # buffer intact, not torn
    assert len(ed._undo_stack) == n    # one command recorded per edit
    assert len(ed._redo_stack) == 0
```
рЯОЩпЄП "The Barrier makes all 50 threads enter `insert` at the same instant вАФ without it they'd take turns and there'd be no real race. The buffer length is exactly 50 and the undo stack has exactly 50 entries: no insert was lost, no buffer was torn, and the history stayed consistent with the text. That's the proof the one lock is sufficient."

>         doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed.upper())
>     def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
>         doc._apply_delete(self._pos, self._length)
>         doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** none worth mentioning вАФ this is exactly what the Command pattern was chosen for. I'd add a thin `uppercase(pos, length)` editor method only if it's part of the public surface.

> **Twist 2 вАФ "Typing 100 characters then one Ctrl-Z should undo all 100, not one."**
>
> Right now each keystroke is its own command, so undo reverses one char. Editors group a run of edits into one undo unit.
>
> рЯОЩпЄП **What you say:** "I'd add a `MacroCommand` вАФ a command that holds a list of child commands and, on undo, reverses them in reverse order. The editor stays oblivious because a `MacroCommand` *is* an `EditCommand`. The grouping policy (time gap, word boundary) lives in a small coalescer in front of `_record`."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class MacroCommand(EditCommand):
>     def __init__(self, commands: list[EditCommand]) -> None:
>         self._commands = commands
>     def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
>         for c in self._commands:
>             c.execute(doc)
>     def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
>         for c in reversed(self._commands):     # reverse order is the key
>             c.undo(doc)
> ```
> **Trade-off:** adds a coalescing policy (when does a group end?) вАФ worth it for real typing UX, overkill if every edit is already a deliberate block operation. This is the Composite pattern layered onto Command.

> **Twist 3 вАФ "Make it collaborative вАФ two people editing the same document live."**
>
> This is the honest boundary of the design. A lock-based, command-stack model assumes one linear history; collaboration has *concurrent, conflicting* histories that must merge.
>
> рЯОЩпЄП **What you say:** "I'd stop reaching for locks вАФ they don't work across clients without freezing the experience. This becomes an Operational Transform or CRDT problem: each client edits locally and we *transform* concurrent operations against each other so everyone converges to the same document. My `EditCommand`s map nicely to OT 'operations,' but undo/redo semantics get much harder (whose undo? undo across merged edits?). I'd scope this as its own design, not a patch on this one."
>
> **The change:** conceptual, not a small diff вАФ swap the single linear `Document` + stacks for per-site operation logs + a transform/merge function (OT) or conflict-free replicated types (CRDT), plus a network sync layer.
> **Trade-off:** enormous jump in complexity (convergence proofs, intention preservation, tombstones). Absolutely the right call for Google-Docs-scale, absolutely the wrong call for the single-user core we were asked to build.

### Hardest follow-ups (distinct from the twists)

> вЭУ "What if the document is 100 MB and edits are in the middle вАФ isn't the flat `str` O(n) per edit a problem?"
> "Yes, at that scale the `str` splice is the bottleneck. I'd swap the `Document`'s buffer for a **rope** (a balanced tree of string chunks) so insert/delete in the middle become O(log n). Crucially, only `_apply_insert` and `_apply_delete` touch the representation, so the change is contained to `Document` вАФ commands, stacks, and the editor are untouched. The Memento would store a structural snapshot rather than a full copy."

> вЭУ "Unbounded history will eventually exhaust memory вАФ how do you cap it?"
> "Cap `_undo_stack` in `_record`: when it exceeds, say, 1000 entries, drop the oldest. Because each command holds only O(edit-size) inverse info, the cap is about *count*, not document size. The dropped command's removed text is GC'd with it. The only subtlety is that very old edits become un-undoable вАФ acceptable and standard. It's a one-line change in the single place that records edits."

> вЭУ "Multiple app servers each holding a copy of the editor вАФ how do you keep them consistent?"
> "An in-process lock protects one process only; it does nothing across servers. For a single authoritative document you'd funnel all edits through one owner (a single writer per document, sticky-routed) and treat other servers as read replicas. For *concurrent* multi-server editing you're back to OT/CRDT with a sync log вАФ the collaborative twist. The command model helps: operations are serializable objects you can ship over the wire."

> вЭУ "An edit is part of a multi-step operation that fails halfway вАФ how do you avoid a corrupt buffer?"
> "Two layers. Within a single command, I order the primitives so a failure can't leave a partial state: `replace` deletes first (which validates), and the subsequent insert at the same position is always in range, so it can't half-fail. For a *compound* macro that could fail mid-way, I'd wrap it so that if any child command raises, I run `undo` on the children that already succeeded (in reverse) before re-raising вАФ an all-or-nothing macro. That's the same reverse-order logic `MacroCommand.undo` already uses, applied as a rollback."

рЯОЩпЄП **Final 30-second summary:** "The key insight is to model every edit as a Command that carries its own inverse, so undo/redo is just two stacks вАФ O(edit-size) memory, unbounded history, no per-keystroke document snapshot. The editor coordinates a `Document` and two stacks and never branches on edit type; `Insert`/`Delete`/`Replace` carry cheap inverses and `SnapshotCommand` is the Memento fallback for un-invertible edits. This is a single-user domain, so concurrency isn't the focus вАФ I keep the core lock-free and add one editor lock via a Template-Method hook purely for consistency if the object is shared, proved by a 50-thread barrier test. New edit kinds are new Command classes with zero core changes, and real collaborative editing is OT/CRDT вАФ a separate design I'd scope on its own, not bolt on."

View problem statement
Problem statement
Design the core of a text editor that supports inserting, deleting, and replacing text, with full undo and redo. The emphasis is on clean object-oriented modeling of reversible operations.

Core Requirements:

Edit: insert text at a position, delete a range, replace a range.
Undo: revert the last edit, restoring the exact previous state.
Redo: reapply the last undone edit; a fresh edit after an undo clears the redo history.
Validation: reject out-of-range positions; report nothing-to-undo / nothing-to-redo clearly.

Additional Considerations:

Undo/redo should be O(1) per step with unbounded history (carry the inverse, not a full snapshot per keystroke).
Single-user editor: concurrency is not the focus, but operations must stay consistent if accessed from multiple threads.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. TextEditor, Document, EditCommand, Memento).
Model each edit as a Command that knows how to execute and undo itself; use a Memento for snapshot fallback.
Discuss design patterns (Command, Memento).
Treat collaborative editing (OT / CRDT) as an extensibility direction, not the core.

# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Text Editor with Undo / Redo.

The "nouns" of the system live here:

* `Document`  вАФ the one mutable thing: a text buffer plus a caret (cursor).
                It exposes two low-level primitives, `_apply_insert` and
                `_apply_delete`, that the Command objects call. Nothing else is
                allowed to touch the raw buffer, so every mutation funnels
                through one validated door.
* `Memento`   вАФ a frozen snapshot of the buffer + cursor. It is the *fallback*
                undo mechanism: for an edit whose inverse is not cheap to carry
                (an opaque whole-buffer transform), we snapshot first and
                restore on undo. The three built-in edits don't need it вАФ they
                carry a cheap inverse вАФ but the snapshot path is real and tested.
* Exceptions  вАФ one base, `EditorError`, with a specific subclass per failure
                mode so callers catch exactly the thing they expect.

рЯОЩпЄП "There's really only one piece of mutable state вАФ the Document. Everything
else (the commands, the editor's two stacks) is bookkeeping around it. So I make
the Document guard its own buffer and expose just two primitives the commands
drive."
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass


# вФАвФА EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
class EditorError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises вАФ callers catch one type."""


class InvalidPositionError(EditorError):
    """A position or length falls outside the buffer (out-of-range edit)."""


class NothingToUndoError(EditorError):
    """undo() was called but the undo stack is empty."""


class NothingToRedoError(EditorError):
    """redo() was called but the redo stack is empty (or a fresh edit cleared it)."""


# вФАвФА MEMENTO: the snapshot value object (Memento pattern) вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Memento:
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # A read-only photograph of the whole document at one instant: the text and
    # where the caret was. Frozen on purpose вАФ once taken, a snapshot can never
    # drift, so restoring it is guaranteed to reproduce the exact past state.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "The Memento is just a frozen pair: the text and the cursor. It's the
    #     'save game' I can always reload to get back an exact previous state."
    text: str
    cursor: int


# вФАвФА DOCUMENT: the single mutable buffer + caret вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
class Document:
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The only object that owns the raw text. It enforces ONE rule: every
    # position touched must land inside the buffer. Because the two primitives
    # `_apply_insert` / `_apply_delete` are the *only* way to change the text,
    # there is exactly one place to get range-checking right.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "The Document is the source of truth. I deliberately gave it tiny
    #     primitives вАФ insert-a-string-here, delete-a-span-here вАФ and let the
    #     Command objects compose them. The editor never pokes the buffer directly."
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        self._text = text
        # The caret sits between characters; valid range is 0..len(text).
        self._cursor = len(text)

    # вФАвФА queries вФАвФА
    @property
    def text(self) -> str:
        return self._text

    @property
    def cursor(self) -> int:
        return self._cursor

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self._text)

    # вФАвФА validation (the one rule this class guards) вФАвФА
    def _validate_position(self, pos: int, length: int = 0) -> None:
        # WHY: a caret position is valid anywhere from 0 (before the first char)
        #      to len (after the last char). A span additionally must not run
        #      off the end. One helper, every primitive reuses it.
        if length < 0:
            raise InvalidPositionError(f"length must be >= 0, got {length}")

        if pos < 0 or pos > len(self._text):
            raise InvalidPositionError(
                f"position {pos} out of range [0, {len(self._text)}]"
            )

        if pos + length > len(self._text):
            raise InvalidPositionError(
                f"span [{pos}, {pos + length}) runs past end {len(self._text)}"
            )

    # вФАвФА low-level primitives (the ONLY things Command objects call) вФАвФА
    def _apply_insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        # WHY: splice the new text in at `pos`. Caret lands just after what we
        #      typed вАФ exactly where a human's cursor sits after typing.
        self._validate_position(pos)
        self._text = self._text[:pos] + text + self._text[pos:]
        self._cursor = pos + len(text)

    def _apply_delete(self, pos: int, length: int) -> str:
        # WHY: cut the span out AND return it. Returning the removed substring is
        #      what lets DeleteCommand carry its own cheap inverse вАФ undo just
        #      re-inserts this exact text.
        self._validate_position(pos, length)
        removed = self._text[pos : pos + length]
        self._text = self._text[:pos] + self._text[pos + length :]
        self._cursor = pos
        return removed

    def _move_cursor(self, pos: int) -> None:
        # WHY: undo restores not just the text but the EXACT caret it had before
        #      the edit, so "exact previous state" is literally true. Validated
        #      like any other position.
        self._validate_position(pos)
        self._cursor = pos

    # вФАвФА Memento hooks (snapshot-based undo fallback) вФАвФА
    def snapshot(self) -> Memento:
        # WHY: capture text + caret as a frozen Memento before an opaque edit.
        return Memento(text=self._text, cursor=self._cursor)

    def restore(self, memento: Memento) -> None:
        # WHY: reload a snapshot wholesale. This is the fallback undo path used
        #      by SnapshotCommand for transforms with no cheap inverse.
        self._text = memento.text
        self._cursor = memento.cursor

# Python 3.11+
"""The Command hierarchy вАФ the pluggable, reversible-operation seam.

Every edit the user can make is modelled as an `EditCommand`: an object that
knows how to `execute` itself on a Document AND how to `undo` itself exactly.
That is the whole trick behind cheap undo/redo вАФ the editor keeps two stacks of
these objects and never reasons about *how* to reverse an edit; each command
already knows.

Two flavours of command live here:

* `InsertCommand` / `DeleteCommand` / `ReplaceCommand` вАФ carry a *cheap inverse*.
  On execute they capture just enough information (the removed substring, the
  inserted length) to reverse themselves in O(edit-size) memory. No
  whole-document snapshot per keystroke.
* `SnapshotCommand` вАФ the Memento-based *fallback* for an edit whose inverse is
  not cheap to carry (an opaque whole-buffer transform). It snapshots the
  document before and after, and undo/redo just restore those snapshots.

рЯОЩпЄП "I make every edit a little object that can run itself forward and backward.
The editor just pushes and pops these вАФ it never writes a single 'if it was an
insert, delete it' branch. Adding a new kind of edit is a new class, nothing in
the editor changes."
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from collections.abc import Callable

from models import Document, Memento


class EditCommand(ABC):
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The abstraction the TextEditor depends on (Dependency Inversion). The
    # editor's undo/redo stacks hold `EditCommand`s вАФ they never know which
    # concrete edit they're reversing. That's the seam: new edit types plug in
    # with zero editor changes.
    @abstractmethod
    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        """Apply this edit to the document (also used to *redo* it)."""
        raise NotImplementedError

    @abstractmethod
    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        """Reverse this edit, restoring the document to its pre-execute state."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class InsertCommand(EditCommand):
    """Insert `text` at `pos`. Inverse: delete the same span back out."""

    def __init__(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._text = text
        self._cursor_before = 0  # captured on execute so undo restores the exact caret

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: remember where the caret was, THEN insert. If the position is bad,
        #      `_apply_insert` raises before we've recorded anything.
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._text)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: the inverse of "insert N chars at pos" is "delete N chars at pos".
        #      We know N = len(text) without storing a snapshot.
        doc._apply_delete(self._pos, len(self._text))
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)


class DeleteCommand(EditCommand):
    """Delete `length` chars at `pos`. Inverse: re-insert the removed substring."""

    def __init__(self, pos: int, length: int) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._length = length
        self._removed = ""  # the inverse info, captured on execute
        self._cursor_before = 0

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: `_apply_delete` returns the exact substring it cut. We keep it вАФ
        #      that string IS our cheap inverse, no document snapshot needed.
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        self._removed = doc._apply_delete(self._pos, self._length)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed)
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)


class ReplaceCommand(EditCommand):
    """Replace `length` chars at `pos` with `text` (a delete + an insert).

    Inverse info is the removed substring; undo reverses the two steps in
    opposite order: pull the inserted text back out, then restore the old text.
    """

    def __init__(self, pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None:
        self._pos = pos
        self._length = length
        self._text = text
        self._removed = ""
        self._cursor_before = 0

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: replace is just two primitives in a row. Doing the delete first
        #      means the insert position is always valid (the buffer only shrank).
        self._cursor_before = doc.cursor
        self._removed = doc._apply_delete(self._pos, self._length)
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._text)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: undo runs the two steps in REVERSE order вАФ first remove the text we
        #      inserted, then put back the text we deleted. Order matters: do it
        #      the other way and the positions no longer line up.
        doc._apply_delete(self._pos, len(self._text))
        doc._apply_insert(self._pos, self._removed)
        doc._move_cursor(self._cursor_before)


class SnapshotCommand(EditCommand):
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The Memento-based fallback. Some edits have no cheap inverse вАФ e.g. "run
    # this opaque transform over the whole buffer". For those we can't carry a
    # small inverse, so we snapshot the document before and after and let undo /
    # redo just restore the right frozen photo. Same EditCommand contract, so it
    # rides the SAME undo/redo stacks as the cheap commands.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "When an edit isn't cheaply reversible, I fall back to the classic
    #     Memento: snapshot before, snapshot after, and undo/redo flip between
    #     the two saved states. It costs whole-document memory, so I only use it
    #     where carrying an inverse isn't possible."
    def __init__(self, transform: Callable[[str], str]) -> None:
        self._transform = transform
        self._before: Memento | None = None
        self._after: Memento | None = None

    def execute(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # WHY: first run computes the transform and remembers both snapshots;
        #      a later redo just restores the cached 'after' вАФ no re-running the
        #      transform, so it works even if the transform isn't repeatable.
        if self._after is None:
            self._before = doc.snapshot()
            new_text = self._transform(doc.text)
            self._after = Memento(text=new_text, cursor=len(new_text))

        doc.restore(self._after)

    def undo(self, doc: Document) -> None:
        # The editor only ever undoes a command it already executed and pushed,
        # so `_before` is always set here; the assert documents that contract.
        assert self._before is not None
        doc.restore(self._before)

# Python 3.11+
"""TextEditor вАФ the orchestrator вАФ and its thread-safe subclass.

The editor owns three things: the one `Document`, an **undo stack**, and a
**redo stack**. Every mutating call follows the same tiny recipe:

    build a Command  ->  execute it on the document  ->  push it onto undo,
    and clear redo (a fresh edit kills the redo future)

`undo` pops the undo stack, runs the command's `undo`, and pushes it to redo;
`redo` is the mirror image. Because the commands carry their own inverses, the
editor contains ZERO "how do I reverse this?" logic вАФ it just moves objects
between two stacks.

Concurrency note (honest): a text editor is a single-user, essentially
single-threaded domain. There's no real contention to design around. But if the
same editor is *touched* from more than one thread, the document and the two
stacks must move together or they'll desynchronise. So the thread-safe version
guards every operation with ONE editor lock via the no-op `_lock` hook. That's
sufficient and correct here; see ¬І9 for why a single lock is the right call and
why real collaborative editing (OT / CRDT) is a different, larger problem.
"""

import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    Document,
    EditorError,
    NothingToRedoError,
    NothingToUndoError,
)
from strategies import (
    DeleteCommand,
    EditCommand,
    InsertCommand,
    ReplaceCommand,
)


class TextEditor:
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The public API. It coordinates the Document and the two command stacks,
    # and it is the only place that decides WHEN redo gets cleared. It never
    # mutates the buffer directly and never branches on edit type вАФ it builds a
    # Command and lets the Command do the work.
    # рЯОЩпЄП "The editor is a thin coordinator. Each edit becomes a Command that
    #     runs and records itself; undo and redo are just popping one stack and
    #     pushing the other. The cleverness is in the Command, not here."
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        self._document = Document(text)
        self._undo_stack: list[EditCommand] = []
        self._redo_stack: list[EditCommand] = []

    # вФАвФА the concurrency hook (Template Method) вФАвФА
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does NOTHING in the clean core вАФ there are zero lock imports in
        #      the logic below. The thread-safe subclass overrides just this one
        #      method with a real lock, so the diff to make the editor safe is a
        #      handful of lines and the core stays readable.
        yield

    # вФАвФА mutating operations: build a command, run it, record it вФАвФА
    def insert(self, pos: int, text: str) -> None:
        # WHY: execute BEFORE recording. If the position is invalid, execute
        #      raises InvalidPositionError and we never touch the stacks вАФ a
        #      rejected edit leaves history exactly as it was.
        with self._lock():
            command = InsertCommand(pos, text)
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._record(command)

    def delete(self, pos: int, length: int) -> None:
        with self._lock():
            command = DeleteCommand(pos, length)
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._record(command)

    def replace(self, pos: int, length: int, text: str) -> None:
        with self._lock():
            command = ReplaceCommand(pos, length, text)
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._record(command)

    # вФАвФА undo / redo: move one command between the two stacks вФАвФА
    def undo(self) -> None:
        # WHY: pop the last edit, reverse it, and park it on the redo stack so a
        #      later redo can replay it. Empty stack is a clear, typed error вАФ
        #      not a silent no-op вАФ so the caller knows there was nothing to undo.
        with self._lock():
            if not self._undo_stack:
                raise NothingToUndoError("there is no edit to undo")

            command = self._undo_stack.pop()
            command.undo(self._document)
            self._redo_stack.append(command)

    def redo(self) -> None:
        # WHY: the exact mirror of undo. Re-executing the command reproduces the
        #      original edit, then it goes back onto the undo stack.
        with self._lock():
            if not self._redo_stack:
                raise NothingToRedoError("there is no edit to redo")

            command = self._redo_stack.pop()
            command.execute(self._document)
            self._undo_stack.append(command)

    # вФАвФА query вФАвФА
    def text(self) -> str:
        with self._lock():
            return self._document.text

    # вФАвФА private helper вФАвФА
    def _record(self, command: EditCommand) -> None:
        # WHY: this is the rule that makes redo behave like every real editor:
        #      a brand-new edit invalidates the "redo future", so we clear it.
        #      Type a character after undoing and you can no longer redo вАФ the
        #      timeline forked.
        self._undo_stack.append(command)
        self._redo_stack.clear()


class ThreadSafeTextEditor(TextEditor):
    # вФАвФА WHY THIS CLASS вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
    # The only change needed to make the editor safe across threads: override
    # the no-op `_lock` with one real lock that wraps each whole operation.
    # The document and the two stacks then always move together вАФ no thread can
    # ever observe a half-applied edit (a torn buffer or a stack that doesn't
    # match the text).
    # рЯОЩпЄП "One lock around each operation. An edit either fully happens or another
    #     thread waits вАФ there's no in-between state to corrupt. I use an RLock
    #     defensively so a future refactor that nests two ops won't self-deadlock,
    #     though with today's code a plain Lock would do."
    def __init__(self, text: str = "") -> None:
        super().__init__(text)
        self._mutex = threading.RLock()

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._mutex:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Text Editor with Undo / Redo.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK".
"""

import threading

from editor_service import TextEditor, ThreadSafeTextEditor
from models import (
    Document,
    InvalidPositionError,
    NothingToRedoError,
    NothingToUndoError,
)
from strategies import SnapshotCommand


def main() -> None:
    # вФАвФА Case 1: the three edits build up a buffer вФАвФА
    ed = TextEditor()
    ed.insert(0, "Hello")
    ed.insert(5, " World")          # append at the end
    ed.delete(0, 6)                 # drop "Hello "
    ed.replace(0, 5, "Earth")       # "World" -> "Earth"
    print(f"Case 1: after insert/insert/delete/replace -> {ed.text()!r}")
    assert ed.text() == "Earth"
    print("Case 1: insert, delete, and replace each edit the buffer -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 2: undo walks history backward, exactly вФАвФА
    ed.undo()                       # undo replace -> "World"
    assert ed.text() == "World", ed.text()
    ed.undo()                       # undo delete  -> "Hello World"
    assert ed.text() == "Hello World", ed.text()
    print(f"Case 2: two undos restore -> {ed.text()!r}")
    print("Case 2: undo reverses edits and restores the exact previous text -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 3: redo replays what undo reversed вФАвФА
    ed.redo()                       # redo delete  -> "World"
    ed.redo()                       # redo replace -> "Earth"
    assert ed.text() == "Earth", ed.text()
    print(f"Case 3: two redos replay -> {ed.text()!r}")
    print("Case 3: redo reapplies undone edits in order -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 4: a fresh edit after undo clears the redo future вФАвФА
    ed2 = TextEditor("abc")
    ed2.insert(3, "d")              # "abcd"
    ed2.undo()                      # back to "abc"
    ed2.insert(3, "X")              # fresh edit -> "abcX", redo timeline forks
    assert ed2.text() == "abcX"
    try:
        ed2.redo()
        assert False, "redo should have been cleared by the fresh edit"
    except NothingToRedoError as e:
        print(f"Case 4: redo after a fresh edit -> {type(e).__name__}")
    print("Case 4: a new edit after undo throws away the redo stack -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 5: out-of-range edits are rejected, history untouched вФАвФА
    ed3 = TextEditor("abc")
    try:
        ed3.insert(99, "z")
        assert False, "should reject out-of-range insert"
    except InvalidPositionError as e:
        print(f"Case 5: insert at 99 into a 3-char doc -> {type(e).__name__}")
    try:
        ed3.undo()                  # nothing was recorded, so nothing to undo
        assert False, "should have nothing to undo"
    except NothingToUndoError as e:
        print(f"Case 5: undo on empty history -> {type(e).__name__}")
    assert ed3.text() == "abc"
    print("Case 5: bad positions raise a typed error and leave history clean -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 6: the Memento fallback вАФ an opaque transform undone by snapshot вФАвФА
    doc = Document("hello")
    upper = SnapshotCommand(transform=str.upper)
    upper.execute(doc)              # snapshot 'hello', jump to 'HELLO'
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"
    upper.undo(doc)                 # restore the frozen 'hello' snapshot
    assert doc.text == "hello"
    upper.execute(doc)              # redo restores the cached 'HELLO' snapshot
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"
    print(f"Case 6: snapshot-based transform undo/redo -> {doc.text!r}")
    print("Case 6: the Memento fallback reverses an edit with no cheap inverse -> OK")

    # вФАвФА Case 7: many threads insert under one lock вАФ no torn buffer, no lost edit вФАвФА
    n = 50
    safe = ThreadSafeTextEditor()
    start = threading.Barrier(n)

    def edit() -> None:
        start.wait()                # all n hit insert at the same instant
        safe.insert(0, "x")         # insert at 0 is always valid

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=edit) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    print(f"Case 7: {n} concurrent inserts -> len={len(safe.text())}, undo_depth={len(safe._undo_stack)}")
    assert len(safe.text()) == n            # every insert landed, none lost
    assert len(safe._undo_stack) == n       # one command recorded per edit
    print("Case 7: the editor lock serializes edits вАФ buffer and history stay consistent -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Text Editor with Undo / Redo.

Covers: each public edit (insert / delete / replace), every typed exception path,
undo-then-redo exactness, undo past the start, the redo-cleared-by-fresh-edit
rule, replace = delete + insert correctness, the Memento snapshot fallback, and a
modest concurrency proof (N threads insert under one lock вАФ no lost edits, no torn
buffer) using a threading.Barrier so all threads race at one instant.
"""

import threading

import pytest

from editor_service import TextEditor, ThreadSafeTextEditor
from models import (
    Document,
    InvalidPositionError,
    Memento,
    NothingToRedoError,
    NothingToUndoError,
)
from strategies import (
    DeleteCommand,
    InsertCommand,
    ReplaceCommand,
    SnapshotCommand,
)


# вФАвФА happy path: one test per public edit вФАвФА
def test_insert_at_position():
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")
    ed.insert(5, ",")
    assert ed.text() == "Hello, World"


def test_delete_range():
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")
    ed.delete(5, 6)                       # remove " World"
    assert ed.text() == "Hello"


def test_replace_range():
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")
    ed.replace(6, 5, "Earth")             # "World" -> "Earth"
    assert ed.text() == "Hello Earth"


def test_text_query_reflects_initial_content():
    assert TextEditor("seed").text() == "seed"


# вФАвФА replace == delete + insert (correctness of the compound command) вФАвФА
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "start,pos,length,text,expected",
    [
        ("Hello World", 6, 5, "Earth", "Hello Earth"),  # same-length swap
        ("abcdef", 1, 3, "X", "aXef"),                   # shrink
        ("abc", 1, 1, "YYYY", "aYYYYc"),                 # grow
        ("abc", 1, 0, "Z", "aZbc"),                      # zero-length == pure insert
    ],
)
def test_replace_equals_delete_then_insert(start, pos, length, text, expected):
    via_replace = TextEditor(start)
    via_replace.replace(pos, length, text)

    via_steps = TextEditor(start)
    via_steps.delete(pos, length)
    via_steps.insert(pos, text)

    assert via_replace.text() == expected
    assert via_steps.text() == expected


# вФАвФА typed exception paths вФАвФА
@pytest.mark.parametrize("pos", [-1, 12])
def test_insert_out_of_range_raises(pos):
    ed = TextEditor("Hello World")        # length 11, valid caret 0..11
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.insert(pos, "x")


@pytest.mark.parametrize("pos,length", [(10, 5), (-1, 2), (0, 99)])
def test_delete_out_of_range_raises(pos, length):
    ed = TextEditor("Hello")
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.delete(pos, length)


def test_replace_out_of_range_raises():
    ed = TextEditor("abc")
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.replace(2, 5, "z")             # span runs past the end


def test_undo_on_empty_history_raises():
    with pytest.raises(NothingToUndoError):
        TextEditor("abc").undo()


def test_redo_on_empty_history_raises():
    with pytest.raises(NothingToRedoError):
        TextEditor("abc").redo()


def test_rejected_edit_leaves_history_untouched():
    ed = TextEditor("abc")
    with pytest.raises(InvalidPositionError):
        ed.insert(99, "z")
    assert ed.text() == "abc"
    with pytest.raises(NothingToUndoError):   # nothing was recorded
        ed.undo()


# вФАвФА undo / redo exactness вФАвФА
def test_undo_then_redo_restores_exactly():
    ed = TextEditor("")
    ed.insert(0, "Hello")
    ed.insert(5, " World")
    ed.delete(0, 6)
    ed.replace(0, 5, "Earth")
    assert ed.text() == "Earth"

    history = ["World", "Hello World", "Hello", ""]
    for expected in history:              # undo all the way back to empty
        ed.undo()
        assert ed.text() == expected

    forward = ["Hello", "Hello World", "World", "Earth"]
    for expected in forward:              # redo all the way forward again
        ed.redo()
        assert ed.text() == expected


def test_undo_past_start_raises_after_draining_history():
    ed = TextEditor("")
    ed.insert(0, "a")
    ed.undo()
    assert ed.text() == ""
    with pytest.raises(NothingToUndoError):
        ed.undo()                          # one step past the start


def test_fresh_edit_after_undo_clears_redo():
    ed = TextEditor("abc")
    ed.insert(3, "d")                      # "abcd"
    ed.undo()                              # back to "abc"
    ed.insert(3, "X")                      # fresh edit forks the timeline
    assert ed.text() == "abcX"
    with pytest.raises(NothingToRedoError):
        ed.redo()                          # the "abcd" redo is gone


def test_undo_restores_exact_cursor():
    ed = TextEditor("abcdef")
    ed.insert(3, "ZZZ")                    # caret now after the insert
    ed.undo()
    # "exact previous state" includes the caret: it was at the end (6) before.
    assert ed.text() == "abcdef"
    assert ed._document.cursor == 6


# вФАвФА Memento snapshot fallback (Document + SnapshotCommand) вФАвФА
def test_document_snapshot_and_restore():
    doc = Document("hello")
    snap = doc.snapshot()
    assert isinstance(snap, Memento)
    doc._apply_insert(5, "!!!")
    assert doc.text == "hello!!!"
    doc.restore(snap)
    assert doc.text == "hello" and doc.cursor == snap.cursor


def test_snapshot_command_undo_redo():
    doc = Document("hello")
    cmd = SnapshotCommand(transform=str.upper)
    cmd.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"
    cmd.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "hello"
    cmd.execute(doc)                       # redo uses the cached 'after' snapshot
    assert doc.text == "HELLO"


# вФАвФА command-level inverse correctness вФАвФА
def test_delete_command_carries_its_inverse():
    doc = Document("abcdef")
    cmd = DeleteCommand(2, 3)              # remove "cde"
    cmd.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abf"
    cmd.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abcdef"


def test_insert_and_replace_commands_round_trip():
    doc = Document("abc")
    ins = InsertCommand(3, "XYZ")
    ins.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abcXYZ"
    rep = ReplaceCommand(0, 3, "Q")
    rep.execute(doc)
    assert doc.text == "QXYZ"
    rep.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abcXYZ"
    ins.undo(doc)
    assert doc.text == "abc"


# вФАвФА modest concurrency proof (Barrier => all threads insert at one instant) вФАвФА
def test_concurrent_inserts_under_lock_lose_nothing():
    """N threads each insert one char under the editor lock.

    The buffer length must equal the number of inserts (no lost update / torn
    buffer) and the undo stack must hold exactly one command per edit.
    """
    n = 50
    ed = ThreadSafeTextEditor()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def attempt() -> None:
        barrier.wait()                     # all n race into insert simultaneously
        ed.insert(0, "x")                  # position 0 is always valid

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert len(ed.text()) == n             # every insert landed
    assert ed.text() == "x" * n            # buffer is intact, not torn
    assert len(ed._undo_stack) == n        # one recorded command per edit
    assert len(ed._redo_stack) == 0        # every edit cleared redo


def test_concurrent_undo_after_inserts_is_consistent():
    """After N inserts, N undos from many threads must drain to empty exactly."""
    n = 30
    ed = ThreadSafeTextEditor()
    for _ in range(n):
        ed.insert(0, "x")
    assert len(ed.text()) == n

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def undo_one() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        ed.undo()

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=undo_one) for _ in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert ed.text() == ""                 # all undone, no double-pop crash
    assert len(ed._undo_stack) == 0
    assert len(ed._redo_stack) == n
